# c-moivkc4y-f8def200d3e2 [live]
> Output contract for review rigs — what does a review-vision/review-charge/review-piece rig produce as its own resolution? Side effects (child cartograph nodes, child review mandates) are clear, but does the rig's writ completion carry a summary, a list of created child writ ids, an analysis report, or simply a bare 'completed' phase with no resolution payload? Affects observability, replay, and any downstream apparatus that wants to know what a review round produced without scanning the writ tree.
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-28T17:03:14.290Z

## c-mod53o6h-5605d3c29086 [concluded]
> Introduce product as a first-class writ type; patron owns all visions (client proxy stays private). Portfolio is implicit (1:1 with guild/patron/all-products) for v0 — revisit if any of those become 1:N.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Design fully resolved across five concluded children (companion-book pattern c-modee576, ladder edges via parentId c-modee5kb, ladder invariants enforced at typed-API c-modee5x4, single-plugin packaging c-modee69x, naming finalized c-modeor2d). Dispatched as mandate w-moepkalv-0ead1599bc23 (codex nexus, complexity 5, posted via nsg commission-post --type mandate; body in post-planning spec format with task manifest). Final shape: @shardworks/cartograph-apparatus registers vision/charge/piece writ types with companion books (visions/charges/pieces) keyed by writ id, exposes typed createVision/createCharge/createPiece API enforcing ladder invariants (vision has no parent; charge.parentId must be vision; piece.parentId must be charge or piece), ships a vision-keeper.md role-file stub. Agent runtime, CLI surfaces, and Reckoner integration are all out of scope for this dispatch — separate follow-on commissions when ready.
Resolved by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Created: 2026-04-24T16:43:35.849Z
Resolved: 2026-04-25T19:04:57.187Z

### c-modee576-7a7f68d66043 [concluded]
> Where do product-specific fields (priority, lifecycle stage, vision text, owner) live — writ body blob, status observation slot, companion book keyed by writ id, or a new typed-payload writ field?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Option 3 (companion book). Plugin owns a typed book per ladder writ type (tentative names: products, capabilities, outcomes), each keyed by writ id — primary key of the doc IS the writ id, matching the Astrolabe PlanDoc precedent. The writ stays generic; typed fields (priority, stage, owner, etc.) live in the companion doc. Plugin exposes a typed API for create/read/update. Vision text lives in writ.body for v0 — no visionRef field needed; can add later non-breakingly if vision text outgrows the writ body. Lifecycle coupling: ProductStage enum includes 'cancelled' (full set: draft | active | sunset | cancelled); typed API transitions both writ.phase and ProductStage together on cancel/sunset. No CDC-delete, no orphan cleanup — the companion doc survives the writ's terminal states and carries domain-level lifecycle in its own stage field. Rejected Option 1 (body blob — not queryable, wrong semantics), Option 2 (status slot — clerk docs explicitly reserve status for side-channel observations, not intrinsic attributes), Option 4 (typed writ payload — framework change with no clear benefit when Option 3 already provides typed queryable data). Caveat: all names in this conclusion (vision, product, capability, outcome, ProductDoc/ProductStage convention) are placeholders — naming finalization tracked in sibling click c-modeor2d.
Created by: 3a73c912-4765-4e2d-814e-e3a8d6f1fdd8
Resolved by: 3a73c912-4765-4e2d-814e-e3a8d6f1fdd8
Created: 2026-04-24T21:03:41.010Z
Resolved: 2026-04-24T21:12:13.054Z

### c-modee5kb-ec3e9a84580b [concluded]
> Ladder edges — parent/child on the writ tree, or typed link kinds (product.has-capability, capability.has-outcome)? Hierarchy fits parent/child; cross-product edges and DAG shapes need links.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Parent/child (writ.parentId) is the primary ladder edge representation; typed link kinds are reserved for non-tree relationships (cross-product dependencies per c-mod53rpa, auxiliary semantic edges). No link kinds registered in v0 — added when concrete use cases arrive. Rationale: clerk.tree() and countDescendantsByPhase already walk parentId for free; existing writ-tree UIs render without modification; Astrolabe's hybrid pattern (parent/child + astrolabe.lifted-from link kind) is the prior-art template. Scope note: this click decides the EDGE MECHANISM only. The number of fixed ladder levels and what nodes exist at each level is a shape question tracked in c-mod53ood. Mandate-attachment rule ('no shortcuts from product to mandate') is a shape concern, cross-referenced to c-mod53ood rather than resolved here. Mandate parenting from petition acceptance (mandate.parentId = outcome / recursion-node / feature) is cross-referenced to the Reckoner subtree under c-mod99ris.
Created by: 3a73c912-4765-4e2d-814e-e3a8d6f1fdd8
Resolved by: 3a73c912-4765-4e2d-814e-e3a8d6f1fdd8
Created: 2026-04-24T21:03:41.483Z
Resolved: 2026-04-24T21:29:14.985Z

### c-modee5x4-757c3b8a264c [concluded]
> Ladder invariants — does the system enforce rules like 'outcome must have a capability parent' and 'capability must have a product parent', or is structure convention-only?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Ladder invariants enforced at the vision plugin's typed API surface, not in WritTypeConfig. Raw clerk.create stays unconstrained — sealed structural constraints on writ types (parentTypes OR allowedChildren, AND- OR OR-composed) are fundamentally closed-world commitments and are open-world-incompatible regardless of which side declares them; either field declared 'for hygiene' by a foundational type owner forecloses uses the owner couldn't predict. Reaffirms the T1 deferral (w-mod644tf) as the load-bearing decision, not a stub. v0 enforcement: vision's createFeature/createPiece/createMandate methods reject bad parents in code; vision is the contract surface for its tree shape. Audit (M3) deferred — see child click. Tree-shape registry (M2) deferred as the principled long-term mechanism if structural enforcement becomes load-bearing — see child click. Rejected M2 for v0 (new construct, design+impl cost without a second consumer demanding it); rejected adding parentTypes/allowedChildren/sealedChildSet to WritTypeConfig (closed-world lock-in regardless of direction or composition).

## c-mod53ood-521f606db4b9 [concluded]
> Design the ladder shape — product is the fixed top; one named first-decomposition type (feature/capability) under product; recursive decomposition node type below that can self-nest to arbitrary depth; mandate attaches at any depth under feature ('no shortcuts from product to mandate'). How many fixed levels, what node types exist at each, and where mandates are allowed to attach.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Ladder shape S2a: fixed top + named first decomposition + recursive node + leaf. Concretely:

- **product** (fixed top) — long-lived; vision; priority. Does not nest.
- **feature** (fixed first decomposition; parent must be product) — patron-contract boundary. Owns acceptance criteria; the unit of patron walkthrough/validation. Does NOT self-nest. If a feature is too big to validate in one walkthrough, split it into two features at the contract layer.
- **piece** (recursive decomposition node; parent must be feature or piece) — internal organization layer. Self-nests to arbitrary depth. No patron contract — invisible to the acceptance/walkthrough surface. Holds the 'how the guild organizes its work to satisfy the contract' content.
- **mandate** (existing leaf) — attaches at any depth under feature: parent must be feature or piece, NEVER directly product (preserves 'no shortcuts from product to mandate').

Two-axis verification falls out cleanly: automated rig-level verification at the mandate/piece layer (contract with the rig); guided patron walkthrough at the feature layer (contract with the patron).

Naming: 'piece' chosen over goal/charge/labor after pressure-testing in real usage contexts (informal reference, nesting, CLI verbs, commit messages, conversational use). Piece is naturally divisible, fits the craft register, has no ghost connotations, and reads frictionlessly across all uses. Collision with Spider's existing 'piece' (atomic sequential task inside a mandate) is being resolved by renaming Spider's piece to 'step' — separate mandate writ to be drafted for that work. Final naming for product/feature/piece (and the XxxDoc convention) still tracked as placeholders in c-modeor2d, but 'piece' for the recursive node is settled here.

Rejected S1 (rigid 4 levels — too inflexible per patron pushback), S3 (free parent/child below feature — fragments tooling, dilutes the patron-contract boundary), S4 (only product fixed — loses the patron-contract layer), and S2b (features self-nest — erodes the patron-contract boundary by creating ambiguous validation surface).

## c-moiu7yc1-b4aa16803c7d [concluded]
> Petitioner runtime overall shape — does decomposition happen via a watching loop that reacts to ladder state, or via a recursive cascade where each layer's elaboration is itself a Reckoner-gated petition that competes for guild resources alongside implementation work?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Recursive cascade pattern (Shape B). Decomposition is work that competes for guild resources via the Reckoner like everything else — there is no rule-based watching loop. Each cartograph-node review is a separate mandate writ posted as a child of the structural cartograph writ, carrying ext.reckoner with priority dimensions chosen for that level. Cascade: vision created → review-vision mandate (Reckoner-gated) → on accept, vision-keeper-provided review-vision rig dispatches via Spider → rig analyzes the vision and creates child charge writs (cartograph) plus a review-charge mandate per charge (Reckoner-gated) → recursion repeats at charge layer producing pieces or mandates → leaf mandates are pure implementation, dispatched as today. Mandates remain the dispatchable unit (no need to extend dispatch to non-mandate writ types, even though Spider's rigTemplateMappings would compose with that). Re-review of a long-lived cartograph node is naturally another mandate — structural writ stays put, work writ is fresh per round. This collapses the earlier rule-based-petitioner-ships-first / LLM-agent-later split (c-mod53q2r): the petitioner role IS the LLM rigs, scheduled through the Reckoner. Packaging revisited: the vision-keeper runtime (rigs, petitioner registrations, the CDC observer that emits initial petitions) lives in a NEW @shardworks/vision-keeper-apparatus plugin, separate from @shardworks/cartograph-apparatus (data layer only — writ types, companion books, typed createX API, ladder invariants). Mirrors how Reckoner scheduling logic is split into a swappable kit-contribution: vision-keeper implementation can be experimented/swapped without disturbing the data layer. The vision-keeper.md role file moves to the new plugin; the cartograph stub goes away. Partially supersedes c-modee69x — the v0 single-plugin decision held while there was no runtime to split; now that there is, the runtime moves out. Source-id grammar follows the registered plugin (vision-keeper.review-vision, vision-keeper.review-charge, vision-keeper.review-piece — or potentially a single vision-keeper.review if a recursive rig works at all layers). Two click-worthy follow-ups identified: initial petition timing (when does the first review-vision petition land for a newly-created vision) and periodic re-evaluation of long-lived visions (v0 lean: manual command).
Supersedes: c-modee69x "Packaging — one 'vision' plugin that owns product/capability/outcome writ types AND the vision-keeper petitioner runtime, or split into 'product-layer' (writ types) + 'vision-keeper' (petitioner)?"

## c-moivk7pd-7bedba64b71a [concluded]
> Lifecycle interactions between Reckoner gating, arbitrary writ types' custom lifecycles, and inter-writ relationships. Reckoner uses new→open as the accept transition — this works cleanly for mandates but assumes the writ type's active phase is named 'open'. How does this generalize to writ types with custom lifecycles (e.g. cartograph charges/pieces have draft|active|... stages and may have non-'open' first active phases)? Second concern: there is currently no way to DRAFT a writ before sending it to the petitioner other than omitting ext.reckoner — once ext is set, the writ is live for Reckoner consideration. This means parent/child or 'follows' dependency relationships cannot be set up before dispatch without risking the target writ being accepted and dispatched before its dependents are wired. Third concern: deadlock potential — if the Reckoner has a WIP limit and accepts a batch of writs that all 'follows'-depend on unaccepted petitions still queued, the accepted writs sit blocked waiting on petitions that may never get capacity to be considered. Need to think through whether a draft-state-before-petition mechanism is needed, whether dependencies should be Reckoner-aware (defer accept until dependencies are clear), and how active-phase naming generalizes per writ-type.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Three lifecycle-interaction concerns resolved with three follow-up children opened.

**Concern 1 (active-phase naming) — already solved at the implementation level.** The Reckoner's resolveActiveTargetPhase walks the writ-type's registered state machine, finds states reachable from the current phase whose classification is 'active', picks the unique candidate, and fail-louds on 0 or 2+. It does NOT hardcode 'open'. For mandate/vision/charge/piece (all six-state new→open→...) it picks 'open' automatically; for a future writ type with new→active it would pick 'active'. The unstated invariant — 'a Reckoner-gated writ type must have exactly one initial→active transition' — is enforced at runtime via fail-loud; no need to lift to writ-type-registration validation in v0. Revisit if/when a second non-mandate writ type opts into Reckoner gating.

**Concern 2 (drafting before petitioning) — workable today, ergonomics gap to close.** The Reckoner gates on ext.reckoner being present (not phase alone), and re-fires on ext.reckoner change (D14). So the draft idiom is: create writ in 'new' WITHOUT ext.reckoner (invisible to Reckoner), set up parent/child + depends-on links, then patch ext.reckoner to publish. Atomic multi-writ setup composes with Stacks transactions. Cross-apparatus authoring (apparatus X creates A, apparatus Y wants B-follows-A) remains an exotic case parked for now. Ergonomics: the publish step needs a first-class API. Follow-up child c-moiwnb9i opens reckoner.petition(writId, ext) helper.

**Concern 3 (WIP-cap deadlock) — Option A: Reckoner-aware dependencies.** Without intervention, the Reckoner can accept a batch of dependent writs whose dependencies are still pending petitions, fill the WIP cap with gated writs in 'open', and starve the dependencies. Resolution: Reckoner reads outbound depends-on links during consideration; if any direct target is non-terminal, defer the petition with defer_reason='dependency' (slots into c-modaqnpt's existing defer machinery). Deferred writs stay in 'new', don't count against WIP, capacity is preserved for the dependencies. Rejected Option B (topological priority bonus — only shifts probability, doesn't eliminate the deadlock), Option C (Spider flags gated writs back to Reckoner — violates c-mof657j4's no-cross-layer-coordination principle), Option D (decline-and-re-emit — pushes bookkeeping onto petitioners, hostile to recursive vision-keeper cascades). Follow-up child c-moiwnmoc opens the design detail (wake-up semantics, target-phase semantics, cycle handling).

**Coupled decision: rename spider.follows → depends-on, Clerk-contributed (unprefixed).** Once the Reckoner reads the dependency link, it's no longer Spider-private. Renaming captures the framework-neutral intent ('B blocked until A reaches terminal-success'). Establishes a broader naming-primacy principle: foundational plugins that own a substrate (Clerk owns the writ graph) get the unique privilege of contributing unprefixed link kinds; everything else is plugin-namespaced (spider.*, astrolabe.*, cartograph.*). Follow-up child c-moiwnzw6 owns the rename + the primacy-principle codification.

Children opened: c-moiwnb9i (reckoner.petition helper), c-moiwnmoc (Reckoner dependency-aware consideration), c-moiwnzw6 (depends-on rename + Clerk naming primacy).

87	> Design the ladder shape — product is the fixed top; one named first-decomposition type (feature/capability) under product; recursive decomposition node type below that can self-nest to arbitrary depth; mandate attaches at any depth under feature ('no shortcuts from product to mandate'). 88	Status: concluded
89	Conclusion: Ladder shape S2a: fixed top + named first decomposition + recursive node + leaf. Concretely:
90	
91	- **product** (fixed top) — long-lived; vision; priority. Does not nest.
92	- **feature** (fixed first decomposition; parent must be product) — patron-contract boundary. Owns acceptance criteria; the unit of patron walkthrough/validation. Does NOT self-nest. If a feature is too big to validate in one walkthrough, split it into two features at the contract layer.
93	- **piece** (recursive decomposition node; parent must be feature or piece) — internal organization layer. Self-nests to arbitrary depth. No patron contract — invisible to the acceptance/walkthrough surface. Holds the 'how the guild organizes its work to satisfy the contract' content.
94	- **mandate** (existing leaf) — attaches at any depth under feature: parent must be feature or piece, NEVER directly product (preserves 'no shortcuts from product to mandate').
95	
96	Two-axis verification falls out cleanly: automated rig-level verification at the mandate/piece layer (contract with the rig); guided patron walkthrough at the feature layer (contract with the patron).
97	
98	Naming: 'piece' chosen over goal/charge/labor after pressure-testing in real usage contexts (informal reference, nesting, CLI verbs, commit messages, conversational use). Piece is naturally divisible, fits the craft register, has no ghost connotations, and reads frictionlessly across all uses. Collision with Spider's existing 'piece' (atomic sequential task inside a mandate) is being resolved by renaming Spider's piece to 'step' — separate mandate writ to be drafted for that work. Final naming for product/feature/piece (and the XxxDoc convention) still tracked as placeholders in c-modeor2d, but 'piece' for the recursive node is settled here.
99	
100	Rejected S1 (rigid 4 levels — too inflexible per patron pushback), S3 (free parent/child below feature — fragments tooling, dilutes the patron-contract boundary), S4 (only product fixed — loses the patron-contract layer), and S2b (features self-nest — erodes the patron-contract boundary by creating ambiguous validation surface).

127	## c-moiu7yc1-b4aa16803c7d [concluded]
128	> Petitioner runtime overall shape — does decomposition happen via a watching loop that reacts to ladder state, or via a recursive cascade where each layer's elaboration is itself a Reckoner-gated petition that competes for guild resources alongside implementation work?
129	Status: concluded
130	Conclusion: Recursive cascade pattern (Shape B). Decomposition is work that competes for guild resources via the Reckoner like everything else — there is no rule-based watching loop. Each cartograph-node review is a separate mandate writ posted as a child of the structural cartograph writ, carrying ext.reckoner with priority dimensions chosen for that level. Cascade: vision created → review-vision mandate (Reckoner-gated) → on accept, vision-keeper-provided review-vision rig dispatches via Spider → rig analyzes the vision and creates child charge writs (cartograph) plus a review-charge mandate per charge (Reckoner-gated) → recursion repeats at charge layer producing pieces or mandates → leaf mandates are pure implementation, dispatched as today. Mandates remain the dispatchable unit (no need to extend dispatch to non-mandate writ types, even though Spider's rigTemplateMappings would compose with that). Re-review of a long-lived cartograph node is naturally another mandate — structural writ stays put, work writ is fresh per round. This collapses the earlier rule-based-petitioner-ships-first / LLM-agent-later split (c-mod53q2r): the petitioner role IS the LLM rigs, scheduled through the Reckoner. Packaging revisited: the vision-keeper runtime (rigs, petitioner registrations, the CDC observer that emits initial petitions) lives in a NEW @shardworks/vision-keeper-apparatus plugin, separate from @shardworks/cartograph-apparatus (data layer only — writ types, companion books, typed createX API, ladder invariants). Mirrors how Reckoner scheduling logic is split into a swappable kit-contribution: vision-keeper implementation can be experimented/swapped without disturbing the data layer. The vision-keeper.md role file moves to the new plugin; the cartograph stub goes away. Partially supersedes c-modee69x — the v0 single-plugin decision held while there was no runtime to split; now that there is, the runtime moves out. Source-id grammar follows the registered plugin (vision-keeper.review-vision, vision-keeper.review-charge, vision-keeper.review-piece — or potentially a single vision-keeper.review if a recursive rig works at all layers). Two click-worthy follow-ups identified: initial petition timing (when does the first review-vision petition land for a newly-created vision) and periodic re-evaluation of long-lived visions (v0 lean: manual command).

145	## c-moivk7pd-7bedba64b71a [concluded]
146	> Lifecycle interactions between Reckoner gating, arbitrary writ types' custom lifecycles, and inter-writ relationships. Reckoner uses new→open as the accept transition — this works cleanly for mandates but assumes the writ type's active phase is named 'open'. How does this generalize to writ types with custom lifecycles (e.g. cartograph charges/pieces have draft|active|... stages and may have non-'open' first active phases)? Second concern: there is currently no way to DRAFT a writ before sending it to the petitioner other than omitting ext.reckoner — once ext is set, the writ is live for Reckoner consideration. This means parent/child or 'follows' dependency relationships cannot be set up before dispatch without risking the target writ being accepted and dispatched before its dependents are wired. Third concern: deadlock potential — if the Reckoner has a WIP limit and accepts a batch of writs that all 'follows'-depend on unaccepted petitions still queued, the accepted writs sit blocked waiting on petitions that may never get capacity to be considered. Need to think through whether a draft-state-before-petition mechanism is needed, whether dependencies should be Reckoner-aware (defer accept until dependencies are clear), and how active-phase naming generalizes per writ-type.
147	Status: concluded
148	Conclusion: Three lifecycle-interaction concerns resolved with three follow-up children opened.
149	
150	**Concern 1 (active-phase naming) — already solved at the implementation level.** The Reckoner's resolveActiveTargetPhase walks the writ-type's registered state machine, finds states reachable from the current phase whose classification is 'active', picks the unique candidate, and fail-louds on 0 or 2+. It does NOT hardcode 'open'. For mandate/vision/charge/piece (all six-state new→open→...) it picks 'open' automatically; for a future writ type with new→active it would pick 'active'. The unstated invariant — 'a Reckoner-gated writ type must have exactly one initial→active transition' — is enforced at runtime via fail-loud; no need to lift to writ-type-registration validation in v0. Revisit if/when a second non-mandate writ type opts into Reckoner gating.
151	
152	**Concern 2 (drafting before petitioning) — workable today, ergonomics gap to close.** The Reckoner gates on ext.reckoner being present (not phase alone), and re-fires on ext.reckoner change (D14). So the draft idiom is: create writ in 'new' WITHOUT ext.reckoner (invisible to Reckoner), set up parent/child + depends-on links, then patch ext.reckoner to publish. Atomic multi-writ setup composes with Stacks transactions. Cross-apparatus authoring (apparatus X creates A, apparatus Y wants B-follows-A) remains an exotic case parked for now. Ergonomics: the publish step needs a first-class API. Follow-up child c-moiwnb9i opens reckoner.petition(writId, ext) helper.
153	
154	**Concern 3 (WIP-cap deadlock) — Option A: Reckoner-aware dependencies.** Without intervention, the Reckoner can accept a batch of dependent writs whose dependencies are still pending petitions, fill the WIP cap with gated writs in 'open', and starve the dependencies. Resolution: Reckoner reads outbound depends-on links during consideration; if any direct target is non-terminal, defer the petition with defer_reason='dependency' (slots into c-modaqnpt's existing defer machinery). Deferred writs stay in 'new', don't count against WIP, capacity is preserved for the dependencies. Rejected Option B (topological priority bonus — only shifts probability, doesn't eliminate the deadlock), Option C (Spider flags gated writs back to Reckoner — violates c-mof657j4's no-cross-layer-coordination principle), Option D (decline-and-re-emit — pushes bookkeeping onto petitioners, hostile to recursive vision-keeper cascades). Follow-up child c-moiwnmoc opens the design detail (wake-up semantics, target-phase semantics, cycle handling).
155	
156	**Coupled decision: rename spider.follows → depends-on, Clerk-contributed (unprefixed).** Once the Reckoner reads the dependency link, it's no longer Spider-private. Renaming captures the framework-neutral intent ('B blocked until A reaches terminal-success'). Establishes a broader naming-primacy principle: foundational plugins that own a substrate (Clerk owns the writ graph) get the unique privilege of contributing unprefixed link kinds; everything else is plugin-namespaced (spider.*, astrolabe.*, cartograph.*). Follow-up child c-moiwnzw6 owns the rename + the primacy-principle codification.
157	
158	Children opened: c-moiwnb9i (reckoner.petition helper), c-moiwnmoc (Reckoner dependency-aware consideration), c-moiwnzw6 (depends-on rename + Clerk naming primacy).

163	### c-moiwnb9i-391a66734c2b [concluded]
164	> Add reckoner.petition(writId, ext) helper that publishes a draft writ by patching ext.reckoner onto an already-created writ. Resolves concern 2 of c-moivk7pd (drafting before petitioning): the existing draft idiom — create writ in 'new' phase WITHOUT ext.reckoner, set up parent/child links and depends-on relationships, then patch ext.reckoner to publish — works mechanically but is undiscoverable. The helper makes the publish step a first-class Reckoner API: takes a writ id and the ext payload (source, priority, complexity), patches the writ via Clerk, fires the existing CDC re-evaluation gate (D14, ext.reckoner change). Atomic multi-writ setup remains: callers wrap create + link + petition() in a Stacks transaction. Worked example: vision-keeper creates a charge writ in 'new' (no ext), creates depends-on link to its review-vision parent, calls reckoner.petition(chargeId, {...}). Open: shape of the ext argument (typed ReckonerExt vs raw object), idempotency on double-publish (no-op if ext already set vs error), patch-vs-replace semantics if ext is partially populated.

166	Conclusion: Design ratified and dispatched as draft commission w-moizema2-f4eee0b6ee44 (awaiting Sean's Oculus review before publish).
167	
168	Final shape: `ReckonerApi.petition()` is overloaded — `petition(request)` (existing, create+stamp) and `petition(writId, extRequest)` (new, stamp-only). The ext-only fields are extracted into a `PetitionExtRequest` interface; `PetitionRequest extends PetitionExtRequest`. The stamp-only form runs identical source-registry, enforceRegistration, partial-priority default-fill, and priority validation as the create+stamp form. Fail-loud guards: existing `ext.reckoner` (one-time act, no overwrite, no deep-compare), and writ not in its writ-type's initial phase (resolved from the type's state machine the same way `resolveActiveTargetPhase` walks for active states — no hardcoded `'new'`). Internal refactor: `petition(request)` delegates to `clerk.post()` + `this.petition(writId, extRequest)`, making the stamp-only form the canonical implementation and the create+stamp form a convenience wrapper. Atomic multi-writ setup composes with Stacks transactions (single `setWritExt()` call at the boundary).

174	> Design Reckoner dependency-aware consideration (Option A) — Reckoner reads outbound depends-on links during consideration; if any direct target is non-terminal, defer the petition with defer_reason='dependency' instead of accepting. Resolves concern 3 of c-moivk7pd (WIP-cap deadlock): without this, the Reckoner can accept a batch of dependent writs whose dependencies are still pending petitions, fill the WIP cap with gated writs sitting in 'open', and starve the dependencies of acceptance capacity. With this, dependents stay in 'new' (don't count against WIP) until their dependencies clear, naturally preserving topological accept order. (3) dangling references — Spider treats missing targets as still-gated; Reckoner should match. (4) cycle handling — Spider already detects cycles in the depends-on graph; Reckoner consideration should also surface cycles before accepting, or rely on Spider to fail-loud post-accept? 178	Final shape: Reckoner reads outbound depends-on links during per-petition evaluation; classifies each target via writ-type config (cleared = terminal+success; failed = terminal+!success; gating = not-terminal); aggregates with failed-precedence (any failed -> defer 'dependency_failed'; otherwise any gating -> defer 'dependency_pending'; all cleared -> proceed). cancelled treated as cleared for v0. No auto-decline on failed deps — petition stays deferred so the writ remains in 'new' (recoverable). Dangling targets treated as gating. No explicit cycle detection — deferral mechanism handles it implicitly; pathologies surface via the staleness diagnostic. Polling-tick wake-up only (matches c-mod9a54n); defer_signal stays reserved. defer_reason enum extended with 'dependency_pending' and 'dependency_failed'.

181	Q2 (phase classification): writ-type config, cancelled-as-success, no auto-decline.
182	Q3 (dangling): defer as gating.

192	> Design the deferred-petition staleness diagnostic — the Reckoner mechanism that surfaces petitions stuck in 'deferred' state for too long (defer_count > N, or first_deferred_at older than T). This is the safety net that the dependency-aware consideration design (parent click) leans on for cycle handling, persistently dangling references, and 'dependency_failed' petitions awaiting petitioner action. Open questions: (1) surface — Lattice pulse, Reckonings log query, dedicated diagnostic writ type, or vision-keeper notification? (2) thresholds — fixed, per-defer_reason ('dependency_failed' escalates faster than 'dependency_pending'), or operator-configurable? (3) action — pure observation (flag for operator), or active intervention (auto-decline after K cycles, auto-nudge priority, etc.)? (4) ownership — does the Reckoner emit the diagnostic itself during its tick, or does a separate apparatus poll the Reckonings log? (5) interaction with petition lifecycle — does diagnostic surfacing change petition state, or is it purely metadata?

198	> Rename spider.follows → depends-on as a Clerk-contributed (unprefixed) link kind, and establish the principle that the Clerk plugin holds primacy of naming for framework-level link kinds. Two coupled decisions: (1) The semantic relationship 'B is blocked until A reaches a terminal-success phase' is fundamental to writ-graph structure, not Spider-internal — Reckoner needs to read it for dependency-aware consideration (sibling click), Spider continues to gate dispatch on it, and any future apparatus reasoning about writ ordering reads the same edge. Renaming to 'depends-on' captures the intent in framework-neutral terms. (2) The Clerk plugin is the framework writ-graph owner and gets the unique privilege of contributing link kinds without a plugin prefix. Plugin-namespaced link kinds (spider.*, astrolabe.*, cartograph.*) remain the default — those plugins own private edges. Unprefixed kinds (depends-on, possibly others later) are Clerk-contributed and represent universal writ-graph semantics every writ-aware operator can rely on. This establishes a 'primacy of naming' convention: foundational plugins that own a substrate get an unprefixed namespace within that substrate. Migration shape: introduce 'depends-on' as a Clerk-registered link kind, add the new kind to Spider's gate-evaluation reader (accept either kind during transition), backfill data via a one-shot CDC migration, retire the spider.follows reads. Open: exact namespace mechanism (does the Clerk register link kinds with a registry the way it registers writ types? or are link kinds free-form strings with the prefix convention purely a soft rule?), audit/enforce no-collision rule between Clerk's namespace and plugin-prefixed kinds, doc placement for the primacy principle (likely guild-vocabulary tome plus framework architecture doc).

Clerk plugin now contributes 'depends-on' as an unprefixed link kind via the carve-out in registerKitLinkKinds (pluginId === 'clerk' may use prefixed or unprefixed form; all other plugins remain prefix-required). One-shot DB migration ran out-of-band (sibling click c-moix6ovo) — 675 rows updated cleanly. Vocabulary tome and aliases registry updated post-dispatch with the primacy principle and the spider.follows → depends-on alias.

### c-modee576-7a7f68d66043 [concluded]
> Where do product-specific fields (priority, lifecycle stage, vision text, owner) live — writ body blob, status observation slot, companion book keyed by writ id, or a new typed-payload writ field?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Option 3 (companion book). Plugin owns a typed book per ladder writ type (tentative names: products, capabilities, outcomes), each keyed by writ id — primary key of the doc IS the writ id, matching the Astrolabe PlanDoc precedent. The writ stays generic; typed fields (priority, stage, owner, etc.) live in the companion doc. Plugin exposes a typed API for create/read/update. Vision text lives in writ.body for v0 — no visionRef field needed; can add later non-breakingly if vision text outgrows the writ body. Lifecycle coupling: ProductStage enum includes 'cancelled' (full set: draft | active | sunset | cancelled); typed API transitions both writ.phase and ProductStage together on cancel/sunset. No CDC-delete, no orphan cleanup — the companion doc survives the writ's terminal states and carries domain-level lifecycle in its own stage field. Rejected Option 1 (body blob — not queryable, wrong semantics), Option 2 (status slot — clerk docs explicitly reserve status for side-channel observations, not intrinsic attributes), Option 4 (typed writ payload — framework change with no clear benefit when Option 3 already provides typed queryable data). Caveat: all names in this conclusion (vision, product, capability, outcome, ProductDoc/ProductStage convention) are placeholders — naming finalization tracked in sibling click c-modeor2d.

Rejected recursive-node alt: waypoint (good register match with cartograph but recursive self-nesting stretches — points don't have parts; framework pattern is evocative-plugin-name-with-functional-content-names anyway, so register-coherence isn't load-bearing here).

## c-mod53ood-521f606db4b9 [concluded]
> Design the ladder shape — product is the fixed top; one named first-decomposition type (feature/capability) under product; recursive decomposition node type below that can self-nest to arbitrary depth; mandate attaches at any depth under feature ('no shortcuts from product to mandate'). How many fixed levels, what node types exist at each, and where mandates are allowed to attach.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Ladder shape S2a: fixed top + named first decomposition + recursive node + leaf. Concretely:

- **product** (fixed top) — long-lived; vision; priority. Does not nest.
- **feature** (fixed first decomposition; parent must be product) — patron-contract boundary. Owns acceptance criteria; the unit of patron walkthrough/validation. Does NOT self-nest. If a feature is too big to validate in one walkthrough, split it into two features at the contract layer.
- **piece** (recursive decomposition node; parent must be feature or piece) — internal organization layer. Self-nests to arbitrary depth. No patron contract — invisible to the acceptance/walkthrough surface. Holds the 'how the guild organizes its work to satisfy the contract' content.
- **mandate** (existing leaf) — attaches at any depth under feature: parent must be feature or piece, NEVER directly product (preserves 'no shortcuts from product to mandate').

Two-axis verification falls out cleanly: automated rig-level verification at the mandate/piece layer (contract with the rig); guided patron walkthrough at the feature layer (contract with the patron).

Naming: 'piece' chosen over goal/charge/labor after pressure-testing in real usage contexts (informal reference, nesting, CLI verbs, commit messages, conversational use). Piece is naturally divisible, fits the craft register, has no ghost connotations, and reads frictionlessly across all uses. Collision with Spider's existing 'piece' (atomic sequential task inside a mandate) is being resolved by renaming Spider's piece to 'step' — separate mandate writ to be drafted for that work. Final naming for product/feature/piece (and the XxxDoc convention) still tracked as placeholders in c-modeor2d, but 'piece' for the recursive node is settled here.

Rejected S1 (rigid 4 levels — too inflexible per patron pushback), S3 (free parent/child below feature — fragments tooling, dilutes the patron-contract boundary), S4 (only product fixed — loses the patron-contract layer), and S2b (features self-nest — erodes the patron-contract boundary by creating ambiguous validation surface).
Resolved by: 3a73c912-4765-4e2d-814e-e3a8d6f1fdd8
Created: 2026-04-24T16:43:36.494Z
Resolved: 2026-04-24T22:10:07.719Z

## c-mod53q2r-e7d500fa4d4a [concluded]
> Should decomposition-ladder authoring be patron-only for v0, or Reckoner-proposes-patron-ratifies from day one?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: B' (anima authors structure without patron approval; patron supplies vision and optionally a few marquee charges) is the destination — this click sits under the autonomous-operation umbrella (c-moa42fn6 Stage 3), so the trajectory's endpoint is autonomy by design. v0 ships as A (patron-only authoring via cartograph CLI) — useful first step that bootstraps the data shape with no agent infrastructure required and produces baseline patron-authored ladders as reference material. Whether B (anima-proposes / patron-ratifies) is a required intermediate stop is left open: if a clean path from A to B' exists without an explicit calibration gate, take it; B exists only as a potential calibration mechanism for measuring agent readiness, not as a destination. Rejected C (sage-on-demand patron-driven assist) — keeps patron in the driver's seat, doesn't graduate toward autonomy, off-trajectory under the autonomous-operation umbrella. Vision-keeper has two distinct responsibilities housed in one agent: rule-based petitioner (watches ladder for ripe pieces, emits petitions to Reckoner — ships when Reckoner is ready, no LLM required) and LLM authoring agent (the A→B'→[skip B?] trajectory). The petitioner role is orthogonal to the authoring trajectory and lands earlier.

## c-moiu7yc1-b4aa16803c7d [concluded]
> Petitioner runtime overall shape — does decomposition happen via a watching loop that reacts to ladder state, or via a recursive cascade where each layer's elaboration is itself a Reckoner-gated petition that competes for guild resources alongside implementation work?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Recursive cascade pattern (Shape B). Decomposition is work that competes for guild resources via the Reckoner like everything else — there is no rule-based watching loop. Each cartograph-node review is a separate mandate writ posted as a child of the structural cartograph writ, carrying ext.reckoner with priority dimensions chosen for that level. Cascade: vision created → review-vision mandate (Reckoner-gated) → on accept, vision-keeper-provided review-vision rig dispatches via Spider → rig analyzes the vision and creates child charge writs (cartograph) plus a review-charge mandate per charge (Reckoner-gated) → recursion repeats at charge layer producing pieces or mandates → leaf mandates are pure implementation, dispatched as today. Mandates remain the dispatchable unit (no need to extend dispatch to non-mandate writ types, even though Spider's rigTemplateMappings would compose with that). Re-review of a long-lived cartograph node is naturally another mandate — structural writ stays put, work writ is fresh per round. This collapses the earlier rule-based-petitioner-ships-first / LLM-agent-later split (c-mod53q2r): the petitioner role IS the LLM rigs, scheduled through the Reckoner. Packaging revisited: the vision-keeper runtime (rigs, petitioner registrations, the CDC observer that emits initial petitions) lives in a NEW @shardworks/vision-keeper-apparatus plugin, separate from @shardworks/cartograph-apparatus (data layer only — writ types, companion books, typed createX API, ladder invariants). Mirrors how Reckoner scheduling logic is split into a swappable kit-contribution: vision-keeper implementation can be experimented/swapped without disturbing the data layer. The vision-keeper.md role file moves to the new plugin; the cartograph stub goes away. Partially supersedes c-modee69x — the v0 single-plugin decision held while there was no runtime to split; now that there is, the runtime moves out. Source-id grammar follows the registered plugin (vision-keeper.review-vision, vision-keeper.review-charge, vision-keeper.review-piece — or potentially a single vision-keeper.review if a recursive rig works at all layers). Two click-worthy follow-ups identified: initial petition timing (when does the first review-vision petition land for a newly-created vision) and periodic re-evaluation of long-lived visions (v0 lean: manual command).
Supersedes: c-modee69x "Packaging — one 'vision' plugin that owns product/capability/outcome writ types AND the vision-keeper petitioner runtime, or split into 'product-layer' (writ types) + 'vision-keeper' (petitioner)?"
Created: 2026-04-28T16:25:36.913Z
Resolved: 2026-04-28T16:25:58.744Z

## c-moiu8pm9-60d67f5af591 [live]
> Initial petition timing — when does the first review-vision mandate land for a newly-created vision? Three candidates: (a) patron posts it manually after createVision (extra step, but explicit and gives patron a chance to seed priority dimensions per vision), (b) cartograph emits it as part of createVision (couples data layer to vision-keeper logic — bad layering), (c) vision-keeper apparatus subscribes to CDC on book.cartograph.visions.created and emits the initial review-vision mandate (clean layering, autonomous, vision-keeper owns its observation surface). Same question recurs for charges and pieces if upstream rigs create them — does the rig that creates a charge also post the review-charge mandate in the same transaction, or is there a CDC observer that fires on any cartograph-node-created event?
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-28T16:26:12.273Z

## c-moiu8tm4-52624db7f9cd [live]
> Periodic re-evaluation of long-lived cartograph nodes — visions especially are durable and the world drifts around them; the canonical Reckoner-contract example (vision-keeper.snapshot) was literally drift detection. v0 lean from this conversation: a manual command (nsg vision review <id> or similar) that posts a review-vision mandate against an existing vision. Future shape (post-v0): scheduled re-review via Clockworks tick or Reckoner deferUntil; or a separate drift-sentinel apparatus that observes the world and emits its own snapshot petitions. Open: does the manual command live in cartograph CLI, vision-keeper CLI, or as a generic 'request review of writ X' surface? Does the same surface work for charges/pieces, or are visions special?
Status: live

## c-moivk7pd-7bedba64b71a [concluded]
> Lifecycle interactions between Reckoner gating, arbitrary writ types' custom lifecycles, and inter-writ relationships. Reckoner uses new→open as the accept transition — this works cleanly for mandates but assumes the writ type's active phase is named 'open'. How does this generalize to writ types with custom lifecycles (e.g. cartograph charges/pieces have draft|active|... stages and may have non-'open' first active phases)? Second concern: there is currently no way to DRAFT a writ before sending it to the petitioner other than omitting ext.reckoner — once ext is set, the writ is live for Reckoner consideration. This means parent/child or 'follows' dependency relationships cannot be set up before dispatch without risking the target writ being accepted and dispatched before its dependents are wired. Third concern: deadlock potential — if the Reckoner has a WIP limit and accepts a batch of writs that all 'follows'-depend on unaccepted petitions still queued, the accepted writs sit blocked waiting on petitions that may never get capacity to be considered. Need to think through whether a draft-state-before-petition mechanism is needed, whether dependencies should be Reckoner-aware (defer accept until dependencies are clear), and how active-phase naming generalizes per writ-type.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Three lifecycle-interaction concerns resolved with three follow-up children opened.

**Concern 1 (active-phase naming) — already solved at the implementation level.** The Reckoner's resolveActiveTargetPhase walks the writ-type's registered state machine, finds states reachable from the current phase whose classification is 'active', picks the unique candidate, and fail-louds on 0 or 2+. It does NOT hardcode 'open'. For mandate/vision/charge/piece (all six-state new→open→...) it picks 'open' automatically; for a future writ type with new→active it would pick 'active'. The unstated invariant — 'a Reckoner-gated writ type must have exactly one initial→active transition' — is enforced at runtime via fail-loud; no need to lift to writ-type-registration validation in v0. Revisit if/when a second non-mandate writ type opts into Reckoner gating.

**Concern 2 (drafting before petitioning) — workable today, ergonomics gap to close.** The Reckoner gates on ext.reckoner being present (not phase alone), and re-fires on ext.reckoner change (D14). So the draft idiom is: create writ in 'new' WITHOUT ext.reckoner (invisible to Reckoner), set up parent/child + depends-on links, then patch ext.reckoner to publish. Atomic multi-writ setup composes with Stacks transactions. Cross-apparatus authoring (apparatus X creates A, apparatus Y wants B-follows-A) remains an exotic case parked for now. Ergonomics: the publish step needs a first-class API. Follow-up child c-moiwnb9i opens reckoner.petition(writId, ext) helper.

**Concern 3 (WIP-cap deadlock) — Option A: Reckoner-aware dependencies.** Without intervention, the Reckoner can accept a batch of dependent writs whose dependencies are still pending petitions, fill the WIP cap with gated writs in 'open', and starve the dependencies. Resolution: Reckoner reads outbound depends-on links during consideration; if any direct target is non-terminal, defer the petition with defer_reason='dependency' (slots into c-modaqnpt's existing defer machinery). Deferred writs stay in 'new', don't count against WIP, capacity is preserved for the dependencies. Rejected Option B (topological priority bonus — only shifts probability, doesn't eliminate the deadlock), Option C (Spider flags gated writs back to Reckoner — violates c-mof657j4's no-cross-layer-coordination principle), Option D (decline-and-re-emit — pushes bookkeeping onto petitioners, hostile to recursive vision-keeper cascades). Follow-up child c-moiwnmoc opens the design detail (wake-up semantics, target-phase semantics, cycle handling).

**Coupled decision: rename spider.follows → depends-on, Clerk-contributed (unprefixed).** Once the Reckoner reads the dependency link, it's no longer Spider-private. Renaming captures the framework-neutral intent ('B blocked until A reaches terminal-success'). Establishes a broader naming-primacy principle: foundational plugins that own a substrate (Clerk owns the writ graph) get the unique privilege of contributing unprefixed link kinds; everything else is plugin-namespaced (spider.*, astrolabe.*, cartograph.*). Follow-up child c-moiwnzw6 owns the rename + the primacy-principle codification.

Children opened: c-moiwnb9i (reckoner.petition helper), c-moiwnmoc (Reckoner dependency-aware consideration), c-moiwnzw6 (depends-on rename + Clerk naming primacy).

### c-moiwnb9i-391a66734c2b [concluded]
> Add reckoner.petition(writId, ext) helper that publishes a draft writ by patching ext.reckoner onto an already-created writ. Resolves concern 2 of c-moivk7pd (drafting before petitioning): the existing draft idiom — create writ in 'new' phase WITHOUT ext.reckoner, set up parent/child links and depends-on relationships, then patch ext.reckoner to publish — works mechanically but is undiscoverable. The helper makes the publish step a first-class Reckoner API: takes a writ id and the ext payload (source, priority, complexity), patches the writ via Clerk, fires the existing CDC re-evaluation gate (D14, ext.reckoner change). Atomic multi-writ setup remains: callers wrap create + link + petition() in a Stacks transaction. Worked example: vision-keeper creates a charge writ in 'new' (no ext), creates depends-on link to its review-vision parent, calls reckoner.petition(chargeId, {...}). Open: shape of the ext argument (typed ReckonerExt vs raw object), idempotency on double-publish (no-op if ext already set vs error), patch-vs-replace semantics if ext is partially populated.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Design ratified and dispatched as draft commission w-moizema2-f4eee0b6ee44 (awaiting Sean's Oculus review before publish).

Final shape: `ReckonerApi.petition()` is overloaded — `petition(request)` (existing, create+stamp) and `petition(writId, extRequest)` (new, stamp-only). The ext-only fields are extracted into a `PetitionExtRequest` interface; `PetitionRequest extends PetitionExtRequest`. The stamp-only form runs identical source-registry, enforceRegistration, partial-priority default-fill, and priority validation as the create+stamp form. Fail-loud guards: existing `ext.reckoner` (one-time act, no overwrite, no deep-compare), and writ not in its writ-type's initial phase (resolved from the type's state machine the same way `resolveActiveTargetPhase` walks for active states — no hardcoded `'new'`). Internal refactor: `petition(request)` delegates to `clerk.post()` + `this.petition(writId, extRequest)`, making the stamp-only form the canonical implementation and the create+stamp form a convenience wrapper. Atomic multi-writ setup composes with Stacks transactions (single `setWritExt()` call at the boundary).

No auto-decline on failed deps — petition stays deferred so the writ remains in 'new' (recoverable). Dangling targets treated as gating. No explicit cycle detection — deferral mechanism handles it implicitly; pathologies surface via the staleness diagnostic. Polling-tick wake-up only (matches c-mod9a54n); defer_signal stays reserved. defer_reason enum extended with 'dependency_pending' and 'dependency_failed'.

Q1 (wake-up): polling-only.
Q2 (phase classification): writ-type config, cancelled-as-success, no auto-decline.
Q3 (dangling): defer as gating.

lastPrompt: let's talk about c-moivkc4y-f8def200d3e2

content.text:
Got the surrounding context loaded. Let me restate the question crisply, then do the analytical work.

## The question, restated

Review rigs (`review-vision`, `review-charge`, `review-piece`) each:
- **Analyze** a cartograph node.
- **Create** child cartograph nodes (charges/pieces) and review-mandates for them.
- **Complete** their own writ.

The side-effects are clear and tree-walkable. The open question is: **what does the rig's own writ-completion carry as a payload?** Specifically, is there a place for the rig's *analysis* — not the structural consequences of it — to live?

## What "review rigs" make different from implementation rigs

For implementation rigs, the artifact is the substance: a commit, a sealed change, a doc. The writ completing bare is fine because the work *is* in the workspace/repo. Walking back from the writ to the artifact is mechanical.

Review rigs invert this. The structural side-effects (charges created, follow-up mandates posted) are *consequences* of an analysis, not the analysis itself. If you only persist the consequences, you've thrown away:

- **Why these breakdowns and not others** (rejected alternatives).
- **How confident the rig was** in this decomposition.
- **What about the parent informed the breakdown** (re-readable context for the next layer's review rig).
- **What concerns or risks were flagged** without rising to first-class child writs.

So the choice is really: **does the rig's analysis need a persistent home, or is the structural outcome enough?**

## Who would actually read the analysis?

This is the load-bearing question. Let me enumerate plausible consumers honestly:

| Consumer | Needs structured output? | Strength of need |
|---|---|---|
| Drift sentinel (diff review N vs N+1) | Yes | Strong — diff over comparable fields is a big win |
| Periodic re-evaluation | Yes (similar to drift) | Strong |
| Staleness diagnostic (`c-moixpj1l`) | No — only timestamps | None |
| Patron walkthrough / inspection | No — prose is fine | Medium, but unstructured |
| Recursive cascade context (review-charge reading parent review-vision's output) | Mixed — prose works; structure is nicer | Medium |
| Petition-queue intelligence | No — reads child mandates' priority instead | None |

**Strongest case is drift detection / re-evaluation** (which is exactly what the sibling `c-moiu8tm4` is about). Everything else is happy with prose or doesn't need the payload at all.

## Options

**A. Bare completion. Side-effects only.**
- Pro: Simplest. Consistent with implementation-rig precedent. Tree is canonical.
- Con: Throws away analysis. Drift detection has nothing to diff. "Why these charges?" is unanswerable post-hoc.
- Con: Recursive cascade loses parent context — review-charge has to re-derive what review-vision was thinking.

**B. Free-form prose conclusion on the writ (single text field on completion).**
- Pro: Minimal mechanism. Captures the rig's narrative.
- Pro: Mirrors how *click conclusions* work — single text field at resolution time. Familiar shape.
- Con: Unstructured — no querying, no mechanical diff.
- Con: Free-form quality drifts.

**C. Workspace artifact (markdown analysis doc, like a code rig commits code).**
- Pro: Zero new mechanism. Rigs already write workspace artifacts.
- Pro: Cheap to read, infinitely flexible.
- Con: Workspace lifecycle is short; if the workspace is GC'd, the analysis is gone.
- Con: Not in the books — out-of-band from the writ graph; harder for apparatus to reach.

**D. Structured ReviewReport in a companion book (cartograph pattern).**
- Pattern: Plugin opens `books.review-reports` keyed by writ id. Rig writes a typed `ReviewReportDoc` on completion. Writ stamps `completed` bare; doc carries the payload.
- Pro: Inherits the established VisionDoc/ChargeDoc/PieceDoc shape — consistent design language.
- Pro: Schema-evolvable. Queryable. Diff-friendly.
- Pro: Drift sentinel and re-evaluation get structured access from day one.
- Con: Schema design cost upfront. Risk of getting ReviewReport shape wrong before we have real consumers.
- Con: Three review layers may want different fields; either over-generalized or three schemas.

**E. Hybrid — bare writ + free-form prose in a small companion doc.**
- Like D but the doc has one field: `narrative: string` (plus metadata: timestamps, rig version, parent-node id). Schema-light.
- Pro: Persistent home for the prose without committing to structured fields.
- Pro: Easy to graduate to D later — add fields to the doc as consumers materialize.
- Con: Still adds a book for v0 with limited mechanical value.

## Recommendation

**Start at E (bare writ + a thin companion doc carrying narrative + metadata), with a clear graduation path to D as consumers land.**

Reasoning:

1. **Persistence matters.** Workspace artifacts (C) lose the analysis to GC; we should treat the review payload as a durable book entry the way we treat cartograph nodes themselves. The recursive cascade in particular needs parent-layer reviews to be reliably readable when a child-layer review rig fires — sometimes weeks or months apart.

2. **Don't over-schematize before consumers exist.** D is appealing on the drift-detection axis, but we don't have a drift sentinel yet. Picking ReviewReport fields now is shape-guessing. A `narrative` field is honest about the current state: prose is what we have, structure is what we'll add.

3. **The companion-doc pattern is already paid for.** The cartograph apparatus established the shape (`books.{name}` keyed by writ id, opened by a plugin, typed accessor). Adding `books.review-reports` to the vision-keeper apparatus is a small repeat of a known move — much cheaper than introducing a different mechanism.

4. **Graduation is non-breaking.** When drift detection lands and wants `flagged_risks: string[]` or `confidence: number` or `decomposition_rationale: string`, those are additive fields on `ReviewReportDoc`. Reviews authored before the field exists either backfill-on-read (parse from narrative) or carry `null` — and the drift sentinel's "compare reviews going forward" semantics are honest.

5. **Aligns with the recursive-cascade story.** When `review-charge` fires, it can call `cartograph.getReviewReport(parentVisionId)` and read the prose — giving the child rig genuine context about why this charge exists in the parent's eyes. That's a real use even before any sentinel materializes.

## Same contract across review-vision / review-charge / review-piece?

Yes, I'd argue. Same shape — `ReviewReportDoc { narrative, parent_node_id, rig_version, completed_at }`. The layer is implicit in which book entry / which parent type the writ targeted. Variation across layers (if it materializes) becomes additive optional fields, not separate doc types.

## Open sub-question worth surfacing

If we go with E or D, **who owns `books.review-reports`?** The vision-keeper apparatus (since it owns the rigs) is the natural choice — the cartograph apparatus stays a pure data layer per `c-moiu7yc1`. That keeps the split clean: cartograph owns the *structural* writs and their docs; vision-keeper owns the *review attempts* and their reports.

## Next steps

If E lands as the direction, I'd open two children:
- **Schema for `ReviewReportDoc` v0** (just `narrative` + metadata, or richer?)
- **Apparatus ownership** (vision-keeper, confirming)

Want me to push on any of those, or pressure-test the recommendation? I'm particularly curious whether you think C (workspace artifact) is worth more weight than I gave it — the "rigs write artifacts, we already have that" appeal is real, and the persistence concern only bites if reviews are actually re-read across long timescales.

content: We're designing the output contract for review rigs (review-vision/charge/piece) under the vision-keeper subtree. I recommended a bare writ plus a thin companion ReviewReportDoc with narrative and metadata. Next: your call on direction or pressure-test. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: Please reason about how the agents which decompose visions and features will do so, and what things they will be considering. It seems like there could be a lot of different approaches, and the 'structured output' would really depend on the approach. It's almost liek structured output would need to capture both the content and a description of the structure, since it depends on what created it. given taht, i'd like to start as minimally as possible here.. What is our minimal viable "persistent reasoning" surface, and what are the upgrade paths as the system evolves

content.text:
Yes — this is exactly the right pressure to apply. Let me actually walk through the approach space, then back into the minimal surface.

## How might a review-vision rig actually decompose a vision?

Imagine the vision is "Nexus enables autonomous agent guilds to ship software with patron oversight." Here are eight genuinely-different approaches a rig could take. Each one produces a different *kind* of intermediate artifact:

| Approach | What the rig considers | Natural artifact shape |
|---|---|---|
| **Capability surface** | What capabilities does this require? Writ system, dispatch, review, observation… | List of capabilities + dependencies |
| **User journey** | What does a patron actually *do*? Post a commission, watch it run, review, dispatch follow-up… | Scenarios + capabilities they imply |
| **Gap-driven** | What's currently broken or missing that blocks the vision? | Gap analysis + remediation per gap |
| **Layer stack** | Foundation → orchestration → observation → human surface | Layer map + responsibility per layer |
| **Risk-first** | What would make this vision fail? | Failure modes + mitigating charges |
| **Experimentally** | Vision as hypothesis; charges as experiments to test it | Hypotheses + experimental program |
| **Pattern-match / template** | This looks like a dev-tools vision; apply standard breakdown | Template + customizations |
| **Socratic / dialectic** | Self-dialogue: what's most important, what's risky, what's foundational? | Reasoning transcript + extracted breakdown |

Different approaches consider *different things* and produce *differently-shaped* intermediate artifacts. A capability-decomposition rig is reasoning about *dependency graphs and interfaces*. A risk-first rig is reasoning about *threats and mitigations*. A user-journey rig is reasoning about *scenarios and paths through the system*.

You're right that a structured output would need to carry both content and an interpretation key for the structure. We don't even know yet which approaches will work — some will be tested, fail, and be discarded. **Locking schema now would amount to picking a winner before the experiment runs.**

## What's actually stable across approaches?

Strip the approach-specific stuff away. What persists *regardless of how the rig thought*?

| Element | Approach-stable? |
|---|---|
| The rig's first-person narrative of what it considered and decided | **Yes** — prose adapts to any approach |
| Identity: which writ, which target node, which rig, which version | **Yes** |
| Timestamps | **Yes** |
| Inputs the rig referenced (writ ids, prior reviews, vision body) | Borderline — could be structured (list of ids) or narrated |
| Structural changes the rig made | **No** — already in writ tree, no need to re-persist |
| Confidence, risk levels, criteria coverage, alternatives considered… | **No** — approach-specific or not always relevant |

So the *genuinely* approach-independent surface is **prose + identity metadata**. Everything else either belongs to a specific approach or is recoverable from the writ graph.

## Minimal viable persistent reasoning

```typescript
interface ReviewReportDoc {
  writId: string;            // primary key — the review rig's writ id
  targetNodeId: string;      // which cartograph node was reviewed
  rigName: string;           // 'review-vision' | 'review-charge' | 'review-piece'
  rigVersion: string;        // for replay / diffing across versions
  completedAt: string;       // ISO timestamp
  narrative: string;         // markdown, free-form
}
```

That's the whole thing. One payload field — `narrative` — plus an envelope. No required structure for what goes IN the narrative; rigs use whatever shape their approach demands.

**Owned by the vision-keeper apparatus** (the rigs' home). Cartograph stays a pure data layer per `c-moiu7yc1`.

**Why this is the right floor:**
- Persistent — survives workspace GC, lives in the books, queryable by writ id.
- Approach-agnostic — no rig is forced to lie about its reasoning to fit a schema.
- Text-diffable — early drift detection can compare narratives even without structured fields (noisy, but not nothing).
- LLM-parseable later — when sentinels emerge, an LLM can extract structured signals from prose. We don't need to anticipate the extraction shape now.
- Companion-book pattern is already paid for — same shape as VisionDoc/ChargeDoc, just thinner.

## Upgrade paths

These are all **non-breaking additions** to the v0 base. Each is triggered by a real need, not anticipated.

**Path 1 — Optional typed fields per consumer.**
First sentinel that lands declares its needs. Drift detection wants `summary: string` and `key_concerns: string[]`? Add them as optional fields. Rigs start filling them. Old reports have null; consumers handle null gracefully ("can't diff this one; needs re-review"). Schema accretes only as load-bearing consumers materialize.

**Path 2 — Approach tagging.**
When we observe approach diversity in practice (one rig family does capability-decomp, another does gap-driven), add `approach: string` (free-form tag) plus `approach_payload: object` for approach-specific structure. Approach-aware sentinels read the payload; approach-agnostic ones stick to narrative. Crucially, this is *bottom-up*: we tag what we're already doing, we don't predict approaches.

**Path 3 — Promote successful patterns.**
If a specific structured pattern (say, "considered alternatives with rejection reasons") proves valuable across multiple sentinels and multiple approaches, promote it from approach-specific to first-class optional field. Backfill where the prose makes it possible; tolerate gaps elsewhere.

**Path 4 — Schema versioning if needed.**
If a non-additive change is ever required, add `schemaVersion: int` and migrate. Probably not needed for years, but the door's open.

## What we explicitly defer

- **Structured criteria coverage** — depends on whether reviews have shared criteria across approaches. They probably don't.
- **Confidence scoring** — only meaningful within some approaches.
- **Considered-alternatives list** — natural to some approaches, foreign to others.
- **Cross-review diffing tooling** — text-diff at v0; structured diff when fields land.

Each of these is a reasonable thing to want eventually. None is reasonable to design now.

## Honest risks of starting this minimal

- **Drift detection ships weaker** at first — text-diff is noisy. The drift sentinel may need to wait for Path 1 to ship cleanly.
- **No cross-review queries** — "show me all reviews with low confidence" is impossible until a confidence field exists. Acceptable; we don't have that query yet.
- **Quality of narrative depends on rig discipline** — agents may write thin narratives. Mitigation: rig prompts can suggest narrative shape ("explain what you considered, what you decided, what you flagged") without enforcing schema.

## What this changes from my previous recommendation

I had Option E as "thin companion doc with narrative + metadata." That holds — but your framing tightens the *justification*. The reason to go thin isn't just "we don't have consumers yet" — it's **schema would falsely commit us to an approach before we know which approaches work.** The narrative field is doing structural-humility work, not just YAGNI.

## Next moves if you want to lock this in

I'd close `c-moivkc4y` with this conclusion (minimal companion doc, narrative + metadata, owned by vision-keeper apparatus, upgrade paths enumerated) and open one child for **Path 1 trigger conditions** — i.e., what concrete consumer need will pull the first structured field into the schema, so we have a clear "we add fields when X" rule rather than letting it accrete arbitrarily.

Sound right, or do you want to push on any of the approaches I sketched? message.content: I think we will need to experiment with approaches. I genuinely don't know. so I think minimal prose-lnly makes sense. my main issues now are naming -- why "narrative", and why are we calling this process "review*

content.text:
Both naming choices were lazy on my part — I imported "review*" from the parent conversation and reached for "narrative" because it sounds prose-y. Neither is right.

## On "narrative"

Wrong because it has a literary register that doesn't match the project's working/instrument vocabulary (clerk, ratchet, cartograph). Also leans toward "story being told" when what we actually want is "the rig's reasoning trace."

Candidates I considered:

| Name | Pull | Push |
|---|---|---|
| `prose` | accurate to form | describes form not function |
| `analysis` | natural English word | biases toward analytical approaches |
| `findings` | review-y register | implies investigation-then-conclusion |
| `assessment` | judgmental register | leans toward "review" framing we're trying to avoid |
| `account` | matches "first-person trace" framing | slightly informal |
| `reasoning` | function-direct, approach-agnostic | none I can find |
| `body` | mirrors writ.body | conflates with input vision text |

**Recommend `reasoning`.** It's the most approach-agnostic option (every approach involves reasoning, regardless of style), function-direct (this is literally the rig's reasoning), and matches what we've been calling this surface in conversation ("persistent reasoning surface"). `analysis` is the runner-up and still acceptable.

## On "review*"

Wrong for stronger reasons:

1. **"Review" looks backward.** It implies inspecting something existing for quality. But these rigs aren't checking work — they're *producing the next layer of structure*. The rig isn't reviewing the vision; it's elaborating it into charges.
2. **Collides with code-review semantics** already in the system (the reviewer rig that QAs implementations). Two different activities sharing a name will breed confusion.
3. **Misses the cartograph register entirely.** The apparatus is `cartograph`, the data lives in books, the metaphor is mapping. The naming should sound cartographic.

The cartograph register is sitting right there. A cartographer **surveys** territory and produces **charts**. Both candidates are good:

| Verb | Reads as | Strength |
|---|---|---|
| `chart-vision` / `chart-charge` / `chart-piece` | "make a chart of this vision" | Same root as `cartograph`. Verb-direct. The output (structural decomposition) IS a chart. |
| `survey-vision` / `survey-charge` / `survey-piece` | "examine this vision and map it" | Emphasizes the *examination* — the actual reasoning work, separate from the structural output. |

The interesting nuance: **chart names the artifact-act, survey names the reasoning-act.** The rig does both — examines the parent, produces children. Either name covers it; they emphasize different ends.

**My recommendation: `chart-vision` / `chart-charge` / `chart-piece`.**

Reasons:
- Same word-root as the apparatus (`cartograph` ↔ `chart`). Verb and noun share lineage.
- Reads as a complete sentence: "the rig charts the vision into charges."
- Re-evaluation case is naturally `re-chart` — drift detection triggers a re-charting of the node.
- The companion doc becomes `ChartingDoc` (the charter's notes), which sits naturally next to VisionDoc/ChargeDoc/PieceDoc in the cartograph apparatus's books.

`survey` is the strong alternative if you want to emphasize the analytical work over the cartographic output. The split would be: *the rig surveys to produce a chart*, and the rig name picks which side of that to foreground.

## Pulled-together v0 shape

```typescript
interface ChartingDoc {
  writId: string;            // primary key — the charting rig's writ id
  targetNodeId: string;      // cartograph node that was charted
  rigName: string;           // 'chart-vision' | 'chart-charge' | 'chart-piece'
  rigVersion: string;
  completedAt: string;
  reasoning: string;         // markdown, free-form
}
```

Apparatus: vision-keeper. Book: `chartings` (or `charting-docs`). Rigs: `chart-vision`, `chart-charge`, `chart-piece`.

Want me to lock this in on the click, or push further on either name first? I'm slightly more confident on `chart*` (rig name) than `reasoning` (field name) — the field name is fine but doesn't have a strong cartograph-register anchor; if you have a better one I'm open.

content.text:
Yeah, you're right — "survey-charge" reads cleanly where "charge-charting" stumbles on the alliteration. Going with `survey-vision` / `survey-charge` / `survey-piece`.

And yes — moving from "chart" to "survey" genuinely opens up the field-name landscape, because we now have a real cartographic-practice register to draw from.

## Field name in the survey register

A surveyor produces a survey, and what they actually write down — both the territory observations AND their reasoning about the decomposition — is most naturally called…

| Name | Pull | Push |
|---|---|---|
| `reasoning` | Function-direct; matches "persistent reasoning surface" framing from this convo | Generic — doesn't borrow strength from the survey register |
| `notes` | True surveyor's term ("field notes"); permissively encompasses observations + reasoning + decisions; reads naturally ("survey notes") | Slightly informal; sounds like marginalia rather than primary content |
| `findings` | Cohesive with survey register; "the survey's findings" is idiomatic | Leans investigative — "what we found" rather than "how we thought" |
| `observations` | Surveyor-natural | Collides with status observations on writs |
| `report` | Survey report is a real noun-pair | Bureaucratic register |
| `account` | "Surveyor's account" works | "Account" overloads with bookkeeping |

**Recommendation: `notes`.**

Reasons:
- Strongest register coherence with `survey` — "survey notes" is the actual term of art for what surveyors record while working.
- Most permissive about what goes inside. Reasoning, observations, considered alternatives, flagged risks, decomposition rationale — all fit naturally under "notes." `findings` and `reasoning` each subtly bias the rig toward a particular kind of content.
- The doc context (`SurveyDoc`) already establishes that these are *survey* notes — we don't need the field name to carry the activity, just the content.
- "Marginalia" worry is overruled by the fact that this IS the surveyor's primary written output. The structural changes (new children) are the chart; the doc is the notes that produced and explain the chart.

Runner-up: `findings`. Slightly more polished register, but the investigative bias is real — it nudges rigs toward "here's what I discovered" framing, which fits gap-driven and risk-first approaches but feels off for capability-decomposition or template-driven approaches.

`reasoning` was the right pick when the doc was register-neutral (`ReviewReportDoc`). Now that the doc is `SurveyDoc`, leaning into the metaphor is stronger.

## Pulled-together shape

```typescript
interface SurveyDoc {
  writId: string;            // primary key — the survey rig's writ id
  targetNodeId: string;      // cartograph node that was surveyed
  rigName: string;           // 'survey-vision' | 'survey-charge' | 'survey-piece'
  rigVersion: string;
  completedAt: string;
  notes: string;             // markdown, free-form
}
```

- Apparatus: vision-keeper
- Book: `surveys` (matching `visions` / `charges` / `pieces` plural pattern)
- Rigs: `survey-vision`, `survey-charge`, `survey-piece`
- Re-evaluation reads as `re-survey` (survey the same node again as world drifts) — clean.

Vocabulary entry candidate: **survey** — a vision-keeper rig's act of examining a cartograph node and producing the next layer of decomposition; produces structural children plus a SurveyDoc capturing the surveyor's notes.

Want me to close the click on this shape and update the vocabulary tome (and aliases registry, since "review*" was used through this whole conversation thread)?

content.text:
Locking in. Let me be honest about what this click actually produces.

## What this click gives us

**Mostly a design constraint, with a small bookkeeping tail.**

The vision-keeper apparatus doesn't exist yet — per `c-moiu7yc1`, it's a future `@shardworks/vision-keeper-apparatus` plugin that hasn't been commissioned. So the SurveyDoc/notes/survey-rig shape is **pre-spec**: it gets folded into that apparatus's commission brief whenever it's drafted, not implemented now.

What this click *does* concretely produce:

| Item | Status | Where |
|---|---|---|
| Conclusion text capturing the design (companion-doc pattern, single `notes` field, envelope metadata, upgrade paths) | To write | `c-moivkc4y` conclusion |
| `survey` vocabulary entry (the activity, the rigs, the doc) | To write | `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md` |
| Aliases for `review-vision` → `survey-vision` etc. (this conversation thread used "review*" extensively, and the parent click `c-moa42rxh` subtree references it throughout) | To write | `docs/future/vocabulary-aliases.yaml` |
| Update to the `vision-keeper.md` placeholder shipped in the cartograph apparatus | To check | `packages/plugins/cartograph/vision-keeper.md` (in the framework repo) — depends what's in the placeholder |

## What this click does NOT trigger

- No commission to dispatch. The vision-keeper apparatus is the load-bearing piece, and it's a separate future commission that will inherit this design.
- No code changes in the cartograph apparatus (already shipped, doesn't deal with surveys).
- No schema migration (no SurveyDoc instances exist anywhere yet).

## Suggested move

1. Conclude the click with the design (notes-field, naming, upgrade paths).
2. Vocabulary tome: add `survey` entry.
3. Aliases registry: add the `review-*` → `survey-*` entries.
4. Quick check on `vision-keeper.md` in the cartograph package — if it mentions "review" terminology, update it; if it's a one-line stub, no-op.

Want me to do all four in a single commit? It's tight, scoped, sanctum-side (the framework repo edit is a small terminology fix, not commissionable). I'll handle the vocabulary work after I peek at the current state of both files.
