toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Vocabulary Alias Registry
#
# Machine-readable companion to `guild-vocabulary.md`. Narrative definitions
# live in the tome; this file exists so humans and tools can resolve a
# historical term to its current canonical form without parsing prose.
#
# When a vocabulary change happens, add an entry here in addition to
# updating the tome. Old clicks, briefs, commits, and session transcripts
# stay truthful to their era — the registry is how downstream readers
# and tools bridge the drift.
#
# Schema
# ------
# aliases:
#   - canonical: <current term>
#     legacy: [<historical term>, ...]
#     relationship: renames | subsumes | drops
#         # renames  — clean swap; old term is obsolete, substitute freely
#         # subsumes — canonical is broader; old term named a narrower
#         #            concept now expressed as a function of canonical
#         # drops    — old term is discontinued with no direct replacement
#     changed_at: <YYYY-MM-DD, optional>
#     note: <short prose explaining the change>
#     tome: <anchor or section title in guild-vocabulary.md, optional>
#     see: [<click id>, ...]  # related clicks, optional
#
# Ordering: most recent changes first. New entries go at the top.

aliases:
  - canonical: depends-on
    legacy: [spider.follows]
    relationship: renames
    changed_at: 2026-04-28
    note: >-
      Link kind for writ-graph dependency edges (source cannot
      advance until target reaches a terminal phase). Renamed from
      spider.follows when the kind became framework-level — the
      Reckoner reads it for petition-consideration gating, the
      Spider reads it for dispatch gating, and Astrolabe writes
      it from observation-lift. Promoted from the Spider plugin's
      kit-contribution to a Clerk-contributed unprefixed kind
      (Clerk owns the writ graph; Clerk gets unprefixed-namespace
      primacy). One-shot DB migration (675 rows) ran 2026-04-28.
    tome: "## Naming primacy"
    see: [c-moiwnzw6, c-moix6ovo]

  # Historical note (2026-04-24): earlier framing (2026-04-23) proposed
  # the Reckoner as a broad command-and-control executive subsuming
  # vision-keeping, overseer pattern, in-flight monitoring, and
  # intervention pulses. That framing was revised the next day — the
  # Reckoner was narrowed to a petition scheduler (accepts petitions
  # from registered petitioners, weighs against priorities and
  # resource constraints, dispatches accepted petitions as writs).
  # Vision-keeper, overseer, and monitoring concepts are now distinct,
  # and interact with the Reckoner (as petitioners) or with the Lattice
  # (as Sentinel pulse-emitters) rather than being Reckoner-internal.
  # No alias is recorded for this revision because no term was renamed —
  # "Reckoner" stayed stable; only its scope narrowed. The tome's
  # Reckoner entry is the current canonical description.

  - canonical: Petition
    legacy:
      - "Brief (as patron input, pre-2026-04-18)"
    relationship: renames
    changed_at: 2026-04-18
    note: >-
      Naming was inverted. "Petition" is the patron's submitted
      request at the front of the pipeline; "Brief" is now reserved
      for the Distiller's polished, structured output artifact.
      Historical clicks and commits using "brief" in the patron-input
      sense are archival.
    tome: "### Petition"
    see: [c-mo4bkd63]

  - canonical: Distiller
    legacy: [BA, PO, "Business Analyst", "Product Owner", BA/PO]
    relationship: renames
    note: >-
      Pre-plan role that consumes a petition and produces a brief.
      Renamed from BA/PO because the essence is distilling a messy
      petition into a clarified, machine-parseable brief.
    tome: "### Distiller"

  - canonical: Sage
    legacy: [analyst, reader-analyst, sage-reader-analyst]
    relationship: renames
    note: >-
      Plan-stage role that consumes a brief and produces a spec.
      Formal identifier remains "sage-reader-analyst" in code until
      migrated.
    tome: "### Sage"

  - canonical: Pulse
    legacy: [missive]
    relationship: renames
    note: >-
      Transmission event through the Lattice. "Pulse" coheres with
      the network metaphor and decouples size from semantics — both
      short signals and payload-bearing completion reports fit.
    tome: "### Pulse"

  - canonical: Lattice
    legacy: [Herald]
    relationship: subsumes
    note: >-
      Herald was a proposed carrier role. When the Lattice settled
      as direct-delivery (no intermediate courier), the carrier role
      collapsed into the apparatus. Herald is preserved in the tome
      under "Dropped" terms in case a channel-specific courier
      concept re-emerges.
    tome: "### The Lattice"

  - canonical: manifest
    legacy: [summon, compose]
    relationship: renames
    note: >-
      Engine name. Rename chain: summon → compose → manifest. Noun
      "Summons" (a formal urgent request for a member's attention)
      was freed by removing "summon" from the engine vocabulary.
    tome: "**Summons**"

+  - canonical: surveyor
+    legacy: [vision-keeper]

+    note: >-
+      Role responsible for surveying cartograph nodes (visions,
+      charges, pieces) and producing the next layer of decomposition.
+      Renamed from vision-keeper because the activity (surveying)
+      defines the role, and because the surveyor works on charges
+      and pieces too — not just visions. Cartograph register: a
+      surveyor surveys territory and produces charts.
+    tome: "### The Surveyor"
+    see: [c-moivkc4y]
+  - canonical: surveying
+    legacy: [vision-keeping, "review (in vision-keeper context)"]
+    relationship: renames
+    changed_at: 2026-04-29
+    note: >-
+      The activity a surveyor performs: examine a cartograph node
+      (vision/charge/piece), reason about decomposition, create

+      Each survey produces a SurveyDoc capturing the surveyor's

+      semantics and implied looked-back inspection rather than
+      producing the next layer).
+    see: [c-moivkc4y]
+  - canonical: survey-vision

+    see: [c-moivkc4y]

+      the substrate plugin owning durable mechanics: SurveyDoc,
+      books.surveys, surveyor registry, CDC observer for the

lines: - **Coinmaster / Purse / Tithe** → cost tracking & token budget allocation (`w-mnszznt5-66d0ec7464bc`) — under umbrella `w-mo0e2m9q` *Unlocking autonomous operation*

- **Petition / Petitioner / Reckoner / Reckonings** → petition scheduler apparatus (`c-mod99ris`) — under umbrella `c-moa42fn6` *Stage 3: Self-commissioning*. The Reckoner accepts petitions from registered petitioners (vision-keepers, tech-debt watchers, laboratory/introspection, etc.), weighs them against guild priorities and resource constraints, and dispatches accepted petitions as writs. The Reckonings book (`c-modc7m16`) is the Reckoner's append-only evaluation log. Supersedes the earlier narrower "internal commission flow" bookmark (`c-mo1mqgqv`, writ `w-mnszzo8h-42137bda6681`).

-- **Vision-keeper** → custodian apparatus for product vision and decomposition ladder (`c-moa42rxh`) — now a petitioner to the Reckoner, not an executive in its own right. One vision-keeper per product.

+- **Surveyor / surveying / SurveyDoc** → the surveyor role + `surveyor-apparatus` substrate + `scaffold-surveyor` default (`c-moa42rxh`). Surveyors examine cartograph nodes (visions, charges, pieces) and produce the next layer of decomposition; each survey produces a SurveyDoc capturing the surveyor's notes. Surveyors emit petitions to the Reckoner when ladder state is ripe. Renamed from vision-keeper (`c-moivkc4y`); apparatus split into substrate + default as a substrate-extension-default move (`c-moje45l8`).

- **Vigil / Sentinel** → reserved labels for watching activities and watcher functions. Distributed across the guild (not exclusive to any one apparatus): Sentinels emit Lattice pulses for patron-facing signals and/or emit petitions to the Reckoner for guild-facing corrective work. Individual Sentinels tracked under their respective design clicks (in-flight monitoring `c-mo1mqgf9`, overseer pattern `c-moaj06ty`, intervention pulses `c-mo1z3teo`).
Other terms in this tome remain latent — they live here until they earn a bookmark or get absorbed into an active inquiry.

lines: A role contract, not an apparatus — any part of the guild that emits petitions into the Reckoner. Petitioners register through the Reckoner's extension point and implement a contract covering: emit-petition (the submission), receive-feedback (declines, deferrals), and lifecycle hooks. Initial petitioner classes:
-- **Vision-keepers** — emit petitions when ladder state marks an outcome ripe for dispatch.
+- **Surveyors** — emit petitions for surveys (decomposition rounds) when cartograph nodes are created or when drift is detected against an existing node.
- **Tech-debt watchers** — emit cleanup petitions from periodic codebase scans.
- **Laboratory / introspection processes** — emit research, analysis, and post-mortem petitions.

- **Sentinel-shaped monitors** — emit petitions for corrective commissions when their watched condition crosses a threshold (distinct from Sentinels that emit patron-facing pulses through the Lattice).

input.old_string:
### The Vision-keeper

The guild's custodian of product vision. Each vision-keeper holds a forward-looking product vision and its **decomposition ladder** (vision → capabilities → outcomes → commissions), maintains the edges between those rungs, and exposes queries: what is ripe for dispatch, which outcomes are starved, what in-flight work maps to which capability. One vision-keeper per product; a guild with multiple products has multiple vision-keepers.

Vision-keepers are **petitioners** — they emit petitions to the Reckoner when their ladder says work is ripe. They do not dispatch work directly; scheduling decisions belong to the Reckoner, which weighs a vision-keeper's petitions against other petitioners (tech-debt watchers, laboratory/introspection, other vision-keepers) and against resource constraints.

Decomposition-ladder authoring (who produces vision → capability → outcome breakdowns — patron-only, or Reckoner-proposes / patron-ratifies) is a separate design question tracked under the vision-keeper subtree. Likely patron-authored for v0, with sage-like assistance as the machinery matures.
input.new_string:
### The Surveyor

The guild's surveyor — a role that examines cartograph nodes and produces the next layer of decomposition. The activity is **surveying**: read the parent node (vision, charge, or piece), reason about how to decompose it, create child writs through the cartograph apparatus, and post follow-up survey petitions for the new children. Each survey round produces a **SurveyDoc** in `books.surveys` capturing the surveyor's notes — a free-form markdown reasoning trace, deliberately approach-agnostic so different surveyor implementations can take different decomposition approaches (capability-decomposition, gap-driven, risk-first, journey-based, etc.) without committing to a shared structured vocabulary.

Surveyors are **petitioners** — they emit petitions to the Reckoner when ladder state says work is ripe (a new cartograph node has been created, or drift has been detected against an existing node). They do not dispatch work directly; scheduling decisions belong to the Reckoner, which weighs a surveyor's petitions against other petitioners and resource constraints.

**Plugin shape (substrate + extension + default).** The `surveyor-apparatus` substrate plugin owns the durable mechanics: SurveyDoc and `books.surveys`, the surveyor registry (kit-contribution surface), the CDC observer that fires the cascade when cartograph nodes are created, the survey-vision / survey-charge / survey-piece rig-name convention, and SurveyDoc stamping on rig completion. Concrete surveyors register via kit contribution and provide rig templates for each cartograph layer. `scaffold-surveyor` is the first concrete surveyor — a minimal default useful for getting surveys flowing, designed to be replaced as approaches are iterated. The shape mirrors the Reckoner's substrate + contributed schedulers + default scheduler.

One surveyor per product (and one product per portfolio in v0). Renamed from vision-keeper because the activity defines the role and because surveyors work on charges and pieces, not just visions. See vocabulary-aliases.yaml for the full alias trail.

lines: **Muster** — the act of assembling members for a commission. Before dispatch, the system musters the required members: checks the roster, verifies availability, confirms the sage is ready, ensures the artificer is active. System mapping: the pre-dispatch validation step. Currently implicit in `send`, but formalizing it as "muster" makes it a named, debuggable phase.

-**Vigil** — the *activity* of watching and waiting, not an actor or construct. Vigil is what is *kept* — the ongoing monitoring itself. A label for the distributed watching functions across the guild: Sentinels keep vigil over the things they watch (writ state, queue depth, daemon health, purse balances, drift from a vision-keeper's ladder, etc.). Vigil is not owned by any single apparatus — an earlier design framing that centralized it in the Reckoner was revised when the Reckoner was narrowed to its petition-scheduler role. Specific Sentinels are designed individually, not as a single monolithic Vigil. Not introduced to code or architecture docs yet.

+**Vigil** — the *activity* of watching and waiting, not an actor or construct. Vigil is what is *kept* — the ongoing monitoring itself. A label for the distributed watching functions across the guild: Sentinels keep vigil over the things they watch (writ state, queue depth, daemon health, purse balances, drift from a surveyor's cartograph, etc.). Vigil is not owned by any single apparatus — an earlier design framing that centralized it in the Reckoner was revised when the Reckoner was narrowed to its petition-scheduler role. Specific Sentinels are designed individually, not as a single monolithic Vigil. Not introduced to code or architecture docs yet.

**Sentinel** — a single scoped watcher. A Sentinel watches one specific thing (writ state, queue depth, daemon health, purse balance, drift from vision-keeper ladder) and acts on its condition in one of two ways: emit a **pulse** through the Lattice for a patron-facing signal, or emit a **petition** to the Reckoner for a guild-facing corrective commission. Plural by design: many Sentinels, each narrow in scope, composed across the guild rather than housed in a single executive. Not introduced to code or architecture docs yet — reserved until individual Sentinel designs firm up under the notification and overseer subtrees.

1	# Coco Log — Standing Research Instrument
2	#
3	# One entry per unit of work handled directly by Coco (the interactive
4	# collaborator) rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission.
5	# Parallel to the commission log — together they capture the full
6	# picture of how work is routed in the system.
7	#
8	# An entry is created for each commit (or tight cluster of commits
9	# toward a single goal) that Coco produces during a session.
10	#
11	# Fields:
12	#   session        — Coco's Claude session ID (join key to archived transcripts)
13	#   date           — date the work was done (YYYY-MM-DD)
14	#   item           — short description of the work (one line)
15	#   commissionable — whether this work could have been an autonomous commission

content.content:
commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: cdd4c6ee-e5d7-4369-8b0e-121521a88243
  date: 2026-04-28
  item: "Resurrected rig-moj1f6m2-3ead937c (writ w-moiyh0jz, Reckoner dependency-aware consideration). Babysitter pid 212246 was a 2.5h-old zombie under daemon pgid (parent never reaped); session ses-moj243gp-9c91fbe3 had been recorded as status='rate-limited' with endedAt 22:37:02, but the implement engine still showed running (Spider's collect step never advanced it). Agent's final assistant turn was end_turn with full work-complete summary; commit b69e68f present in draft (reckoner: dependency-aware consideration gate). SQL surgery (db backup nexus.db.bak-coco-rig-resurrect-2-20260428-2238): session status rate-limited→completed with output set to the agent's final summary; implement engine running→completed with synthetic ImplementYields {sessionId, sessionStatus: 'completed'}; review engine's givensSpec.testCommand stripped (replaced with _coco_test_skipped marker pointing to c-moj7h6r7) to dodge the babysitter.test.ts hang during review's mechanical checks (build still runs). nsg crawl one dispatched review; the new reviewer session id was registered (ses-moj7lcus-67534320) but Animator is paused on rate-limit until 22:52:03Z (triggered by the implement session at 22:37:03Z), so the reviewer babysitter won't spawn until the pause clears. Rig will progress naturally once Animator unpauses."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: cdd4c6ee-e5d7-4369-8b0e-121521a88243
  date: 2026-04-29
  item: "Diagnosed Animator/Spider rate-limit-rejection contract bug, fixed it directly in the framework, and shepherded all three rigs to completion. The bug: animate()'s rate-limit pre-check rejection path returns a synthesized rate-limited handle but explicitly does not write a SessionDoc — yet the engine has already returned {status:'launched', sessionId} so the rig records the sessionId on its attempt; Spider's tryCollect later does sessionsBook.get(sessionId), gets null, silently continues, and the engine wedges forever on a ghost session. Both queued sessions from yesterday (ses-moj7pnb1 revise for rig-moj12h4o, ses-moj7lcus review for rig-moj1f6m2) were stuck in this state. Workaround: synthesized the missing rate-limited SessionDocs in SQL — Spider's existing tryCollect rate-limited path then ran handleEngineFailure (rateLimited=true) → engine pending + holdReason='animator-paused' → hold cleared (Animator running) → fresh attempts dispatched naturally. Both replays succeeded: rig-moj1f6m2 sealed cleanly (commit 0e1e81f, dependency-aware consideration gate). rig-moj12h4o's seal hit a rebase conflict against the freshly-landed deep-link work and produced a SealRecoveryYields graft (manual-merge + retry-seal), but my nsg-crawl-one process owned the in-memory pendingGrafts when it queued — daemon's spider didn't see it. Manually instantiated the graft engines into the rig record; daemon picked up seal-manual-merge (mender anima reconciled the conflict), then seal-retry pushed cleanly to main as 7bed456 (feat(reckoner): periodic tick replaces the CDC handler). Per Sean's directive, captured the bug as click c-mojcxzsq under c-moizr765 and patched it directly: animator.ts rejection branch now writes a terminal rate-limited SessionDoc via recordSession() before returning; result promise awaits the write so the engine's collect step on the next crawl tick is guaranteed to see the doc. Two existing tests flipped from no-doc/no-emission to write-and-emit assertions; one neighbor test gained an await handle.result to keep the now-non-no-op SessionDoc write from racing fixture teardown. Verified all 25 framework packages build clean and ~3471 tests pass with --test-timeout=30000 (the babysitter.test.ts 'hang' is actually the VS Code auto-attach debugger injecting --test-timeout=0 into spawned node processes — under a normal shell all 17 babysitter tests run in 4 seconds; that's environmental context for c-moj7h6r7, not a separate bug). Cherry-picked the fix onto a fresh origin/main (avoiding a stale local petitioner-registration.md conflict) and pushed as efd5b0a. Concluded c-mojcxzsq with the SHA and a note that it was directly fixed."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 6e969859-fe2a-4a3b-8365-5847d3e9202d
  date: 2026-04-29
  item: "Worked the deferred-petition staleness diagnostic click (c-moixpj1l) end to end. Drafted the design across five axes (surface, thresholds, action, ownership, lifecycle) in scratch, iterated with Sean through three architectural tensions: discovered the Reckonings book design explicitly rejects named events in favor of auto-wired CDC, that there's no existing writ-side reckoner-status snapshot (Sean introduced one), and that the journal is event-shaped not tick-shaped per the existing design (no rework needed). Sean reframed all three: snapshot lives at writ.status['reckoner'] maintained via a Reckoner-kit CDC handler on book.reckoner.reckonings.created, named events deferred to a sibling click (c-moj6mjep, opened), staleness flag fires only on dependency_failed at 1 tick. Vocabulary: 'stalled' chosen over 'stale' (off — implies neglect-by-time) and 'stuck' (collides with writ-phase 'stuck'). Also delivered a Reckoner-strategy survey doc (.scratch/reckoner/strategy.md) showing the apparatus is NOT out of date — there's a tightly-coupled Apr 28 design burst with ~5 commissions either freshly sealed or in-flight (w-moiy8hkv periodic tick, w-moiyh0jz dependency-aware consideration both still open). Methodology mea culpa captured: when surveying apparatus state, always cross-reference click conclusions against nsg writ show for dispatch ids — the conclusion-with-writ-id pattern is the join key. Posted the brief as draft commission w-mojdwk37-84a1937ecaf8 (codex nexus), wired depends-on link to w-moiyh0jz, concluded c-moixpj1l. Updated the briefs skill to use 'depends-on' instead of 'spider.follows' (link kind renamed to Clerk-contributed unprefixed kind in the recent depends-on rename commission)."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

Rigs renamed review-* → survey-* (cartograph register; chart-* rejected for charge-charting awkwardness); role renamed vision-keeper → surveyor (activity defines role; works on visions/charges/pieces). Plugin shape adopts substrate+extension+default pattern mirroring Reckoner: surveyor-apparatus (substrate) + scaffold-surveyor (default) + future contributed surveyors via kit. Opened five follow-up clicks: c-moje41iq (dynamic rig-template registration — how kit-contributed templates reach Spider; cross-refs c-mo1mqfsa subtree), c-moje45l8 (packaging amender, supersedes c-moiu7yc1's one-plugin packaging decision), c-moje49pk (substrate-vs-implementation scope detail), c-moje4dj9 (default scaffold-surveyor design), c-moje4foc (plural-surveyor selection — parked as future). Vocabulary tome rewrote 'The Vision-keeper' → 'The Surveyor' with substrate/extension/default framing; aliases registry got entries for surveyor/surveying/survey-vision/charge/piece/surveyor-apparatus."

diff --git a/.claude/skills/briefs/SKILL.md b/.claude/skills/briefs/SKILL.md
index 91a4f6b6..d300f481 100644
--- a/.claude/skills/briefs/SKILL.md
+++ b/.claude/skills/briefs/SKILL.md
@@ -89,7 +89,7 @@ Other sections appear when the work warrants them — e.g., "Substrate changes"
 
 The direct-to-system workflow gives you two dispatch modes:
 
-- **Draft (`--draft`)** — the default when any of the following apply: the brief is part of a multi-commission batch, depends on other writs (via `spider.follows`), or Sean hasn't yet seen the final body. Sean reviews in Oculus; publish via `nsg writ publish --id <writ-id>` when ready.
+- **Draft (`--draft`)** — the default when any of the following apply: the brief is part of a multi-commission batch, depends on other writs (via `depends-on`), or Sean hasn't yet seen the final body. Sean reviews in Oculus; publish via `nsg writ publish --id <writ-id>` when ready.
 - **Open (no `--draft`)** — appropriate when the brief was fully worked out in chat, is a single unit, has no dependencies, and Sean has signaled he wants it to go immediately. The writ enters the dispatch queue at post time.
 
 When in doubt, use `--draft`. A draft is cheap to review and publish; an already-dispatched writ is harder to retract.
@@ -135,11 +135,11 @@ Underlying CLI: `bin/commission.sh` wraps `nsg commission-post`. Always prefer t
 
 ### Multi-commission batches
 
-When dispatching multiple related commissions (e.g., a decomposed refactor with dependencies), post all of them as drafts, wire the `spider.follows` links between them, then leave them draft for Sean's Oculus review. Publish the leaves last, once Sean has ratified the batch. Link syntax:
+When dispatching multiple related commissions (e.g., a decomposed refactor with dependencies), post all of them as drafts, wire the `depends-on` links between them, then leave them draft for Sean's Oculus review. Publish the leaves last, once Sean has ratified the batch. Link syntax:
 
-    nsg writ link --source-id <follower> --target-id <prerequisite> --kind spider.follows --label follows
+    nsg writ link --source-id <dependent> --target-id <prerequisite> --kind depends-on --label depends-on
 
-Both `--label` and `--kind` are required — `--label` is the casual relationship name; `--kind` is the load-bearing registered link type that Spider's dispatch logic reads.
+Both `--label` and `--kind` are required — `--label` is the casual relationship name; `--kind` is the load-bearing registered link type that Spider's dispatch logic and the Reckoner's dependency-aware consideration both read.
 
 ## Post-dispatch bookkeeping
 
@@ -165,5 +165,5 @@ For multi-commission batches, conclude the parent click once (naming all dispatc
 - **Leaking sanctum references into the brief.** `.scratch/...` paths, sanctum doc paths, experiment directories. Dead links from the artificer's perspective. See "Stay inside the target repository" above.
 - **Stripping click references.** Don't try to make briefs self-contained by inlining the substance of their source clicks — that's the sage's job. Briefs reference; specs inline.
 - **Skipping the wrapper script.** Always go through `bin/commission.sh` for consistency with the dispatch flow.
-- **Forgetting `--label` on a `spider.follows` link.** The `nsg writ link` command requires both `--label` (casual name) and `--kind` (registered load-bearing type). Passing only `--kind` fails.
+- **Forgetting `--label` on a `depends-on` link.** The `nsg writ link` command requires both `--label` (casual name) and `--kind` (registered load-bearing type). Passing only `--kind` fails.
 - **Forgetting to conclude the parent click.** Leaves the design click sitting in `live` indefinitely, pretending there's still active design work when the work is actually in flight as a commission.

input.command: nsg click show c-moiu8pm9

content.text:
## c-moiu8pm9 — Initial petition timing

Created during the `c-moivk7pd` lifecycle-interactions session two days ago, when we were nailing down how Reckoner gating composes with arbitrary writ types' lifecycles. The click predates this conversation's rename, so it's phrased in the old vocabulary (`review-vision` → now `survey-vision`).

### The question

A brand-new vision gets created — say, you draft a product vision and run something like `nsg vision create`. The vision now exists as a writ + VisionDoc in `books.visions`. Now what?

The cascade we designed in `c-moiu7yc1` says: vision created → survey-vision mandate (Reckoner-gated) → on accept, surveyor rig fires → creates charges + posts follow-up survey-charge mandates → recursion downward.

But **something has to post that first survey-vision mandate.** It doesn't materialize by magic. The question is: who?

### Three candidate answers in the click

| Option | Mechanism | Pro | Con |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(a)** Patron posts manually | After `nsg vision create`, patron runs a separate `nsg vision survey <id>` (or similar) to dispatch the initial survey | Explicit; patron controls timing and can seed priority per vision | Extra step; easy to forget; doesn't compose with autonomous operation |
| **(b)** Cartograph emits during `createVision` | The typed `createVision` API writes the vision AND posts the survey-vision petition in the same transaction | One-step from patron's perspective | **Couples cartograph (pure data layer) to surveyor concepts** — cartograph would need to know rig names, priority defaults, etc. Violates the c-moiu7yc1 layering decision |
| **(c)** Surveyor-apparatus subscribes to CDC | Surveyor-apparatus runs a CDC observer on `book.cartograph.visions.created`; when a vision lands, observer posts the initial survey-vision petition | Clean layering — cartograph knows nothing about surveyors. Autonomous: works for any vision regardless of who created it | Slightly indirect — petition-post decoupled from the create call |

### The recursion question

Same shape recurs at lower layers: when a `survey-vision` rig creates child charges, **does that rig post the follow-up `survey-charge` petitions itself**, or does the **same CDC observer** fire on `book.cartograph.charges.created` and post them automatically?

- **Rig-posts-its-own-followups** — survey-vision rig creates charges + posts survey-charge petitions in the same transaction. More explicit; rig knows what it just created.
- **CDC-observes-all-creations** — substrate observes ALL cartograph node creations and posts initial surveys for any new node, regardless of who created it. Uniform pattern.

### Relevant context to factor in

- **Vision stage lifecycle.** Per `c-modee576`, ProductStage enum is `draft | active | sunset | cancelled`. So there's already a stage signal for "this vision is ready to be acted on" — not just "this vision was created." That nudges option (c) toward observing *stage transitions* rather than raw creation.
- **The Reckoner draft idiom from `c-moivk7pd`.** A writ can sit in 'new' WITHOUT `ext.reckoner` (invisible to Reckoner), have its links/dependencies wired, then get published via `reckoner.petition()` patching `ext.reckoner`. This means the substrate could observe the *publish* step rather than the create step — and surveys themselves could be drafted-then-published if there's a reason to defer.
- **Patron-only authoring in v0** (`c-mod53q2r` conclusion). For v0, the patron authors the cartograph ladder; charges and pieces aren't created by rigs yet. So the recursion question is *deferred work* — only the vision-creation case actually fires in v0.

### What's loose / under-considered

A few things I notice when reading the click that don't feel fully addressed:

1. **Draft visions exist.** A vision can land in 'draft' stage. Should those trigger surveys? Probably not — the patron drafted but hasn't said "go." So if (c), the observer should fire on `stage: draft → active` transitions, not on creation.
2. **Re-survey trigger.** The same CDC mechanism could in principle observe drift signals to re-trigger surveys on existing nodes (`c-moiu8tm4` territory). Worth knowing whether the initial-survey observer and the re-survey observer are the same machinery or different.
3. **Patron seeding priority.** Option (a)'s real virtue isn't just "explicit step" — it's that the patron can set per-vision priority dimensions at petition time. Under (c), the substrate has to use a default priority scheme. We may want a way for the patron to attach priority hints to the vision itself, which the substrate then reads when emitting the petition.

### My read

**Option (c) is right at the layering level — pure data layers shouldn't post petitions.** The interesting design questions are *what* the observer triggers on (creation vs stage transition vs ext-publish) and *how priority gets seeded* (substrate default vs patron-attached hint).

For v0, I'd lean: substrate observes `stage: draft → active` transitions on visions; uses a substrate-default priority unless the VisionDoc carries an explicit priority hint. Charges/pieces deferred since v0 doesn't have rig-driven creation yet.

But that's me racing ahead. Want to start somewhere different — maybe push back on whether (c) is even right, or talk about what the patron's authoring flow actually looks like before we lock the trigger semantics?

input.command: nsg click show c-mojeikyh

content.text:
Read it. The disk-authoring flow makes sense and yes, it forces the issue — surveys can't be coupled to the vision writ's lifecycle when re-applies are first-class.

## Two real concerns here, let me address them separately

### Concern 1: CDC must watch creates AND updates

Yes, fully agree. The substrate's observer fires on:
- `book.cartograph.visions.created` — first apply (initial survey)
- `book.cartograph.visions.updated` — Nth apply (re-survey)

The signal is the same: "this vision changed; structure may need to change." Cheap re-surveys are fine — the surveyor itself decides whether the change warrants new structure. A re-survey on a typo-fix can produce notes that say "no structural changes; vision drift was cosmetic" and create no children. Same for charges and pieces if/when the recursion case lights up.

This collapses `c-moiu8tm4` (periodic re-evaluation) too — the same CDC mechanism handles initial and re-evaluation since "patron edits and re-applies" is the periodic-re-eval mechanism for v0. Drift sentinels can come later.

### Concern 2: "the writ body is gonna kinda suck"

This is the right thing to push on, but I think the answer is **don't make it a mandate** — make it its own writ type.

You said "secondary type of write" which is ambiguous between (a) a mandate that happens to be about surveying and (b) a different writ type called survey. I think (b) is meaningfully better, and it dissolves the trivial-body problem.

Look at how `writ.body` works across existing types:

| Writ type | Body is... | Set when |
|---|---|---|
| mandate | the brief (instruction to the rig) | creation |
| vision | the vision prose | creation/apply |
| charge | the charge prose | creation |
| piece | the piece prose | creation |

For mandates, body = "what to do." For ladder writs, body = "what this is." Surveys naturally fall on the ladder side — the survey **is** an examination, and its content **is** the examiner's notes. So:

- `survey-vision` / `survey-charge` / `survey-piece` writ types (or one `survey` type with a layer field — design knob)
- `writ.body` = the notes the rig writes (markdown, free-form)
- `writ.parentId` = the surveyed cartograph node (vision/charge/piece)
- `SurveyDoc` companion = envelope metadata only (rigName, rigVersion, completedAt, targetNodeType — the stuff that's not the content)
- Lifecycle: `new → open → completed/failed/...` (Reckoner-gated like any other writ type per `c-moivk7pd`)

The rig now has substantive content to write *into* the writ. The body isn't "survey vision vis-asksdf-123" — that's implicit in the type + parentId. The body becomes "Read the vision. The product wants X. Three natural decomposition axes: A, B, C. I chose B because... created charges ch-foo, ch-bar, ch-baz." First-class content where the rig actually deposits its work.

### The catch: this reopens `c-moiu7yc1`

That click concluded with "Mandates remain the dispatchable unit (no need to extend dispatch to non-mandate writ types, even though Spider's rigTemplateMappings would compose with that)." Going the typed-survey route specifically extends dispatch to a non-mandate type.

It's a small amendment — Spider's dispatchable-type set grows by survey-vision/charge/piece (or one `survey` type). The compose-with-that note in the original conclusion suggests this was anticipated as fine; the only reason to keep mandates as the sole dispatchable type was "no need yet." Now there is a need.

This is actually the same shape as `c-moje45l8` (packaging amender) — `c-moiu7yc1`'s conclusion is mostly right but one specific decision needs amending. Worth a sibling supersedes click, or possibly fold into `c-moje45l8` since both amend the same parent.

## Resulting shape

```
Patron edits vision.md + sidecar
   │
   ▼
nsg vision apply <slug>
   │
   ▼
cartograph creates/updates vision writ + VisionDoc
   │
   ▼
[CDC: book.cartograph.visions.created OR .updated]
   │
   ▼
surveyor-apparatus observer fires:
  • creates survey-vision writ (body empty, parentId = vision)
  • stamps ext.reckoner
   │
   ▼
Reckoner accepts (new → open)
   │
   ▼
Spider dispatches the surveyor's survey-vision rig
   │
   ▼
rig reads vision, reasons, creates child charges (cartograph),
    posts follow-up survey-charge writs (recursion),
    fills survey-vision writ.body with notes,
    completes
```

Each layer (charge, piece) follows the same shape — apply-to-cartograph isn't the trigger for those (only visions go through `nsg vision apply`); rig-creates-child-cartograph-node is. The CDC observer fires for those creations identically.

## Stage gating still helps

Worth keeping the stage-transition idea even with this shape: substrate observes only when stage is `active`. A `draft` vision that gets applied still becomes a writ but doesn't trigger a survey. When the patron transitions stage to `active` (via apply with stage change in sidecar), the observer fires.

Or simpler v0: `nsg vision apply` always triggers a survey; if you don't want one, don't apply yet. We can add stage-gating later when draft-without-surveying becomes a real workflow need.

## Suggested moves

1. **Conclude `c-moiu8pm9`** with "(c) — substrate observes cartograph CDC on creates and updates; collapsed with `c-moiu8tm4` per the c-mojeikyh subsumption."
2. **Conclude `c-moiu8tm4`** with the same — the apply CLI is the periodic-re-eval mechanism for v0.
3. **Open a new click** (or fold into `c-moje45l8`) amending `c-moiu7yc1`'s "mandates-only" decision: surveys are typed writs, not mandates; Spider's dispatchable set grows.
4. **Update `c-moivkc4y`'s conclusion**? — it's already concluded so we can't edit. The conclusion mentions SurveyDoc carries the `notes` field; with this new design, notes move to `writ.body` and SurveyDoc shrinks to envelope-metadata-only. Either supersedes the SurveyDoc shape, or we can argue the spirit is preserved (substrate-owned, approach-agnostic, single field).

The fourth one is the awkward bookkeeping move. Want to push on the typed-writ-vs-mandate question more before locking, or are we converged enough to start the supersedes/conclude work?

lastPrompt: I think C is 'rightish', but theres more to consider. Take a look at c-mojeikyh, just authored and sheds light ont he authoring flow. patron edits a file, then runs cli to add/update in stacks basical…

message.content:
do (1) and (2). Three is not a change in what i was thinking... somewhere we had already discussed that multiple writ types can be riggable. I think there's too much churn between concurrent design sessions. I'd like to kinda start over this whole section of the design -- cartograph, visioning, and petitioning for the reckoner for cartograph artifacts, etc.

Can you draft a full design document of these systems by consuming and synthesizing everything in the clicks so far, any relevatn scratch docs, and the acutal state of the system. Include a table with a short summary of what is already shipped (based on commit logs, closed writs, etc.). Then layout all of the "finished design" that could be implemented right now because it is solid enough and/or doesn't touch remaining open questions. Finally, lay out sections for the open questions and what our current thinking is.

I think the goal will be to dispatch what we can, and recreate a new click tree that is more clear.

content.text: Doing (1) and (2) first.

> Design the Reckoner — petition scheduler that accepts petitions from registered petitioners (vision-keepers, tech-debt watchers, laboratory/introspection, etc.), weighs them against guild priorities and resource constraints (concurrency, token budget), and determines which petitions get dispatched as writs
Status: live
Supersedes: c-mo1mqgqv "Design first-class internal commission flow (Petition)"
Created: 2026-04-24T18:40:18.581Z

## c-moa42rxh-d965072428ba [live]
> Design the vision-keeper role — the agent/apparatus that holds a forward-looking product vision and decomposes it into commissionable work
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-22T13:51:35.909Z

### c-mod53o6h-5605d3c29086 [concluded]
> Introduce product as a first-class writ type; patron owns all visions (client proxy stays private). Portfolio is implicit (1:1 with guild/patron/all-products) for v0 — revisit if any of those become 1:N.
Status: concluded

Companion doc convention is XxxDoc matching the PlanDoc precedent: VisionDoc / ChargeDoc / PieceDoc. Companion books: visions / charges / pieces. Plugin package: @shardworks/cartograph-apparatus — made-up instrument (parsable as carto + graph, the device that records maps); pairs as a true cousin of astrolabe (both charting instruments serving complementary planning roles — astrolabe for the sages, cartograph for the vision-keeper). Rejected recursive-node alt: waypoint (good register match with cartograph but recursive self-nesting stretches — points don't have parts; framework pattern is evocative-plugin-name-with-functional-content-names anyway, so register-coherence isn't load-bearing here).

### c-mod53ood-521f606db4b9 [concluded]
> Design the ladder shape — product is the fixed top; one named first-decomposition type (feature/capability) under product; recursive decomposition node type below that can self-nest to arbitrary depth; mandate attaches at any depth under feature ('no shortcuts from product to mandate'). How many fixed levels, what node types exist at each, and where mandates are allowed to attach.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Ladder shape S2a: fixed top + named first decomposition + recursive node + leaf. Concretely:

- **product** (fixed top) — long-lived; vision; priority. Does not nest.
- **feature** (fixed first decomposition; parent must be product) — patron-contract boundary. Owns acceptance criteria; the unit of patron walkthrough/validation. Does NOT self-nest. If a feature is too big to validate in one walkthrough, split it into two features at the contract layer.
- **piece** (recursive decomposition node; parent must be feature or piece) — internal organization layer. Self-nests to arbitrary depth. No patron contract — invisible to the acceptance/walkthrough surface. Holds the 'how the guild organizes its work to satisfy the contract' content.
- **mandate** (existing leaf) — attaches at any depth under feature: parent must be feature or piece, NEVER directly product (preserves 'no shortcuts from product to mandate').

Includes a t3 task to chase down a potential collision: astrolabe currently registers a writ type called 'piece' (atomic-step sense) that would conflict with cartograph's 'piece' (recursive ladder node sense); if the registration still exists, it gets renamed to 'step' to match the spider-side execution-layer rename in b553e49.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Cartograph scaffold landed cleanly (commit f69f1c6, 9 files, +2110 lines). Apparatus at packages/plugins/cartograph/ contributes vision/charge/piece writ types via Clerk's open registry, opens three companion books (visions/charges/pieces) under owner id 'cartograph', exposes a typed CartographApi enforcing parent invariants (vision has no parent; charge.parentId must be a vision; piece.parentId must be a charge or piece). createX methods write writ + companion doc inside one stacks.transaction for atomicity; transitionX methods couple writ.phase and doc.stage. Six-state mandate-clone lifecycle without childrenBehavior cascade. Notable t3 outcome: astrolabe piece-collision check was a no-op — astrolabe registers only 'step' and 'observation-set' (the spider piece→step rename in b553e49 had already cleared the namespace). Vision-keeper.md is a placeholder; agent runtime is a future commission. CLI follows in w-moera46h-272e31c94197 (now unblocked since the spider.follows dependency is satisfied).

##### c-moerc0vf-efc862919540 [concluded]
> Cartograph CLI commission dispatched as w-moera46h-272e31c94197 (codex nexus, complexity 3). Surfaces vision/charge/piece authoring as CLI subcommands following existing per-plugin patterns (nsg writ / nsg click / nsg plan model). Brief is intent-focused; planner derives subcommand structure (per-type top-level vs nested), argument shapes, and stage-transition handling. Linked via spider.follows to the cartograph scaffold writ w-moepkalv-0ead1599bc23 — Spider holds dispatch until scaffold completes.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Cartograph CLI shipped (commit a04cd6c1, sealed via w-moera46h). Adds 15 plugin-contributed tools (3 types × 5 ops) at packages/plugins/cartograph/src/tools/, registered via apparatus supportKit.tools. Decisions: three top-level groups (nsg vision/charge/piece) following framework's per-entity-top-level convention; five ops each (create, show, list, patch, transition); short-prefix id resolution via clerk.resolveId on every id-bearing flag; Zod-enum-validated --phase and --stage (both required) on transition tools; --codex the only patchable companion-doc field; --format text|json on read tools only; callableBy: ['patron'] on every tool; creates land at phase:new/stage:draft with no auto-transition. All routes through the typed cartograph API so ladder invariants hold. Test coverage: 15-tool matrix with happy paths, schema contracts, prefix resolution, error wrapping, format parity, lifecycle round-trip. v0 patron authoring loop is closed: patron can author the full vision/charge/piece ladder from the command line without throwaway scripts.
Created by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Created: 2026-04-25T19:53:43.275Z
Resolved: 2026-04-28T15:54:23.422Z

### c-mod53ood-521f606db4b9 [concluded]
> Design the ladder shape — product is the fixed top; one named first-decomposition type (feature/capability) under product; recursive decomposition node type below that can self-nest to arbitrary depth; mandate attaches at any depth under feature ('no shortcuts from product to mandate'). How many fixed levels, what node types exist at each, and where mandates are allowed to attach.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Ladder shape S2a: fixed top + named first decomposition + recursive node + leaf. Concretely:

- **product** (fixed top) — long-lived; vision; priority. Does not nest.
- **feature** (fixed first decomposition; parent must be product) — patron-contract boundary. Owns acceptance criteria; the unit of patron walkthrough/validation. Does NOT self-nest. If a feature is too big to validate in one walkthrough, split it into two features at the contract layer.
- **piece** (recursive decomposition node; parent must be feature or piece) — internal organization layer. Self-nests to arbitrary depth. No patron contract — invisible to the acceptance/walkthrough surface. Holds the 'how the guild organizes its work to satisfy the contract' content.
- **mandate** (existing leaf) — attaches at any depth under feature: parent must be feature or piece, NEVER directly product (preserves 'no shortcuts from product to mandate').

# Reckoner — current state and forward strategy

## Headline finding

**The Reckoner is not out of date. It is actively being built.** The visible "stubs" in `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/` (the hardcoded `'accepted'` in `evaluateScheduler`, the comment "v0 of the CDC handler does not emit deferred rows") are *intentional placeholders* for two commissions currently in-flight. The implementation lag is expected and is closing imminently.

Where I went wrong in the previous response: I read the live code, saw the placeholders, and concluded the apparatus was behind the design. What I missed was that two open writs are actively replacing those placeholders. Survey first, alarm later.

## Current state

### Recently shipped (sealed, code in tree)

| Writ | Title | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|
| w-mohuvk8x | Clerk plugin-keyed `ext` slot | Petition data layer |
| w-mohuvn8h | Reckoner core: registry/config/helpers/types | Kit-static petitioner registry, source-id grammar, decline-reason enum, accept/decline-only CDC handler |
| w-mohuvshq | Vision-keeper worked example | Reference petitioner |
| w-moepkalv | Cartograph apparatus scaffold | Vision/charge/piece writ types + companion books |
| w-moera46h | Cartograph CLI | Patron authoring loop closed |
| w-moix2b56 | `spider.follows` → `depends-on` rename | Framework-level link kind, Clerk-namer-primacy principle |
| w-moix4pe8 | Clockworks kit-contributed standing orders | Substrate for kit-shipped tick |
| w-moiy7bmo | Scheduler kit-contribution registry | Pluggable scheduler interface + two defaults |
| w-moizema2 | `reckoner.petition()` stamp-only overload | Draft-then-publish ergonomics |

### In-flight (open phase, code not yet merged)

| Writ | Title | What it ships |
|---|---|---|
| **w-moiy8hkv** | Periodic tick for the Reckoner | Replaces the CDC-only handler with a tick-based scheduler. Each tick: re-resolve scheduler config, query held petitions, build SchedulerInput, transition writs per decision, append Reckonings rows. Also: removes the always-approve stub. |
| **w-moiyh0jz** | Reckoner dependency-aware consideration | Reads outbound `depends-on` links during per-petition evaluation; classifies targets via writ-type config; emits **deferred** rows with `deferReason: 'dependency_pending' \| 'dependency_failed'` (extending the enum). Settles cycle-handling by deferral. |

These two are the actual gap-closers between the architecture doc and the live code. **Both must land before the staleness commission (this conversation's click) can dispatch.**

### Concluded clicks awaiting commission

**None.** Every concluded click in the Reckoner subtree has either been dispatched (most of them) or has had its conclusion record explicit dispatch via existing in-flight writs. There is no backlog of "designed but not commissioned" work.

### Live clicks (design in progress)

Eight live clicks remain in the Reckoner subtree. Triaged by readiness:

| Click | Title | Readiness |
|---|---|---|
| **c-moixpj1l** | Deferred-petition staleness diagnostic | **Converging now** (this conversation). Ready to commission once the in-flight w-moiyh0jz lands. |
| c-moiu8pm9 | Initial petition timing — when does first review-vision mandate land for a new vision? | Three options enumerated in click body; the conclusion-favorite is (c) vision-keeper subscribes to CDC on `book.cartograph.visions.created`. Tractable design conversation; could close in one session. |
| c-moiu8tm4 | Periodic re-evaluation of long-lived cartograph nodes | v0 lean already in click body (manual `nsg vision review <id>` command). Could close in one session. |
| c-moivkc4y | Output contract for review rigs | Affects observability/replay; tractable but blocked behind vision-keeper apparatus runtime existing. |
| c-moivkfgb | Priority dimension cascade — bound child priorities by parent? | Click body already says "v0: skip the bounding mechanism." Effectively pre-concluded; one session to ratify. |
| c-mod9a9un | Multi-product / plural-petitioner priority allocation | Future work; no current pressure (single-product guild today). Hold. |
| c-mod9ab0i | Petition dedup and conflict | Future work; designed-out per c-mod9a48y context. Hold. |
| c-mohd0luw | Petitioner-claim calibration loop | Cross-cuts laboratory + Reckoner; calibration data needs first to accumulate. Hold. |

### Parked clicks

Five parked clicks, all with explicit unblock conditions:

| Click | Title | Unblock condition |
|---|---|---|
| c-moixb74x | Operator-configurable Reckoner tick cadence | Real operator need surfaces (multi-environment tuning, cost-driven slowdowns, calibration loop wanting backpressure) |
| c-mof65was | WIP-ceiling shape (single number vs per-petitioner vs priority-weighted) | Operational pressure on the v0 single-number cap |
| c-mod53p9w | Dogfood Nexus as the first product | After authoring machinery is settled |
| c-mod53rbz | How is Nexus's first product-vision captured | After dogfooding decision lands |
| c-mod53rpa | Cross-product dependency edges in writ data model | When second product emerges |

## Why "out of date" was the wrong framing

The pattern that made the apparatus *look* out of date:

1. **Architecture docs settled before implementation** — the Reckonings book design (`docs/architecture/reckonings-book.md`) and apparatus contract (`docs/architecture/apparatus/reckoner.md`) describe the full deferred-row model with rich `deferReason` metadata. The live code only emits `accepted`/`declined`. The **gap is intentional**: design first, implementation second, in tight succession. The CDC-handler-only v0 was deliberately scoped to ship a working spine before the tick-based replacement landed.

2. **Multiple commissions in-flight at once** — the April 28 design burst (yesterday in absolute terms) generated a tightly-coupled batch: the Reckoner.petition() helper, the dependency-aware consideration design, the depends-on rename, the staleness diagnostic, and the periodic tick. Some shipped same-day; others are still working through the build pipeline. A snapshot taken mid-burst sees a transient gap.

3. **No persistent design-without-commission backlog** — every concluded design has a commission writ id in its conclusion. The conclusion field carries the dispatch status as load-bearing data. Reading conclusions front-to-back makes this clear; reading code-only makes it look like designs are evaporating.

The takeaway for future surveys: **always cross-reference click conclusions against `nsg writ show <id>` for the dispatched commission**. The conclusion-with-writ-id pattern is the join key.

## Forward strategy

### Immediate (this session / next session)

1. **Converge `c-moixpj1l` (this click)** — staleness design. Capture in `staleness.md`, then commission. Adds `depends-on` link to **w-moiyh0jz**.

2. **Watch w-moiy8hkv and w-moiyh0jz to completion.** These are the two open commissions blocking the staleness work and most other Reckoner-dependent design. No action needed unless they stall.

### Near-term (next 1–3 sessions)

3. **Close c-moivkfgb** (priority dimension cascade) — the click body already says "v0: skip." Ratify and conclude in a brief session; no commission needed (decision is "no mechanism").

4. **Close c-moiu8tm4** (periodic re-evaluation of cartograph nodes) — v0 lean already names the manual command shape. One session to ratify, then commission a small CLI addition.

5. **Close c-moiu8pm9** (initial petition timing) — three options enumerated; favored option named. One session to confirm, then a small commission for the vision-keeper-side CDC observer.

6. **Close c-moivkc4y** (review-rig output contract) — needs the vision-keeper-apparatus package to exist first. May need an upstream commission to scaffold that package before this design can land. **Open question for Sean: is the vision-keeper apparatus scaffold queued anywhere, or is it an unstated next-up?**

### Holding pattern

7. **Park c-mod9a9un, c-mod9ab0i, c-mohd0luw** — no current pressure; they wait for a triggering condition (multi-product reality, observed dedup conflicts, accumulated calibration data). Track as "live but holding" — they're not blocking anything.

### Maintenance

8. **Update click parent (c-mod99ris) status** — the umbrella Reckoner click is still `live`. Every direct child decision is concluded. The umbrella may be ready to conclude with a short summary pointing at the in-flight implementing writs and the remaining children. Worth a quick decision in a future session.

## Recommended commission posting sequence

Once the staleness design converges:

```
[currently open]
w-moiy8hkv  Periodic tick                          ← in flight
w-moiyh0jz  Dependency-aware consideration         ← in flight, depends-on rename satisfied

[next, in this order]
1. <staleness>         depends-on -> w-moiyh0jz
2. <init petition>     depends-on -> vision-keeper-apparatus scaffold (if not yet posted)
3. <periodic review>   no dependencies; can post any time
```

## Action items / open questions for Sean

- **Vision-keeper apparatus scaffold** — the petitioner-runtime split (c-moiu7yc1) said the runtime moves to a new `@shardworks/vision-keeper-apparatus` plugin separate from `@shardworks/cartograph-apparatus`. Has that been commissioned anywhere I missed? If not, it's the unstated prerequisite for several downstream clicks.

- **c-mod99ris umbrella** — concur on concluding once the in-flight writs land?

- **c-moivkfgb / c-moiu8tm4 / c-moiu8pm9** — these can probably each close in a quick session given how settled the click bodies already are. Worth queuing for a focused "close out the easy ones" session?

lastPrompt: do (1) and (2). Three is not a change in what i was thinking... somewhere we had already discussed that multiple writ types can be riggable. I think there's too much churn between concurrent design se…

1	# `@shardworks/vision-keeper-apparatus`
2	
3	The Vision-keeper is the **canonical worked-example petitioner** from
4	the Reckoner contract document
5	(`docs/architecture/petitioner-registration.md` §11). It exists to
6	exercise every contract surface — kit declaration, source stamping,
7	dimension claims, complexity, payload, labels, withdraw, and standing-
8	order feedback — against a real consumer, and to give every future
9	petitioner an in-tree reference implementation to model from.
10	
11	This v0 commission ships the **petitioner side only**:
12	
13	- An apparatus exposing three caller-facing methods —
14	  `submitDriftSnapshot()`, `submitElaborationNudge()`, and
15	  `superseded()` — surfaced under `VisionKeeperApi`.
16	- A typed `VisionSnapshotPayload` shape (the `visionId`,
17	  `snapshotTimestamp`, `visionVsRealityDelta`, `metricValues` fields
18	  the brief enumerates) that the keeper auto-fills before stamping it
19	  onto `ext.reckoner.payload`.
20	- A `petitioners` kit contribution (declaring the
21	  `vision-keeper.snapshot` source) consumed by the Reckoner at
22	  apparatus startup and sealed at `phase:started`.
23	- The auto-supersede invariant: at most one outstanding petition per
24	  `visionId`. Emitting a second snapshot for the same vision while one
25	  is still outstanding withdraws the prior writ before posting the
26	  new one.
27	- A decline-feedback relay (`vision-keeper-on-decline`) operators wire
28	  via a Clockworks standing order — see "Wiring the decline-feedback
29	  relay" below.
30	
31	Out of scope for this commission (separate follow-up work):
32	
33	- The vision-artifact storage, the drift-detection runtime, and the
34	  rig that processes vision-keeper writs.
35	- Multi-vision orchestration (multiple keeper apparatuses, per-vision
36	  factories, vision registries). v0 supports the labels surface for
37	  multi-instance discrimination — see the per-call `visionId` argument.
38	
39	The Reckoner now ships a CDC approval handler (the always-approve
40	v0 stub): a vision-keeper petition lands in `new`, the handler picks
41	it up via the Phase 2 watcher on `clerk/writs`, and the writ
42	transitions to the type's active state (`open` for mandate). The
43	keeper does not need to drive that transition itself.
44	
45	The apparatus declares `requires: ['reckoner']` and
46	`recommends: ['clockworks']`. Without Clockworks the keeper still
47	emits and withdraws petitions cleanly; only the decline-feedback
48	channel needs the standing-order substrate.
49	
50	See: `docs/architecture/petitioner-registration.md` (the load-bearing
51	contract document) and the sibling Reckoner README for the underlying
52	helper API.
53	
54	---
55	
56	## Installation
57	
58	```json
59	{
60	  "dependencies": {
61	    "@shardworks/vision-keeper-apparatus": "workspace:*"
62	  }
63	}
64	```
65	
66	Add `vision-keeper` to `guild.json`'s `plugins` array. The Reckoner
67	must already be declared as a plugin (the keeper hard-requires it).
68	
69	```json
70	{
71	  "plugins": ["clerk", "reckoner", "vision-keeper"]
72	}
73	```
74	
75	## Emitting a drift snapshot
76	
77	```typescript
78	import type { VisionKeeperApi } from '@shardworks/vision-keeper-apparatus';
79	import { guild } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
80	
81	const keeper = guild().apparatus<VisionKeeperApi>('vision-keeper');
82	
83	const writ = await keeper.submitDriftSnapshot({
84	  visionId: 'product-vision-2026',
85	  title: 'API ergonomics drift detected at 04:00 UTC',
86	  body: 'The `/v2/orders` endpoint now requires three round-trips to '
87	      + 'complete what the vision document specifies as a single call.',
88	  visionVsRealityDelta: {
89	    promised: 'one-call order placement',
90	    observed: 'three sequential calls',
91	  },
92	  metricValues: { ordersPerSecond: 12.3, p95LatencyMs: 410 },
93	});
94	// writ is in `phase: 'new'` with:
95	//   ext.reckoner.source     = 'vision-keeper.snapshot'
96	//   ext.reckoner.priority   = drift-default dimensions (see below)
97	//   ext.reckoner.complexity = 'bounded'
98	//   ext.reckoner.payload    = the typed VisionSnapshotPayload
99	//   ext.reckoner.labels     = { 'vision-keeper.io/vision-id': 'product-vision-2026' }
100	```
101	
102	**Drift-default dimensions** (overridable per-call via the request):
103	
104	| Dimension        | Value                                  |
105	| ---------------- | -------------------------------------- |
106	| `visionRelation` | `'vision-violator'`                    |
107	| `severity`       | `'serious'`                            |
108	| `scope`          | `'major-area'`                         |
109	| `time`           | `{ decay: true, deadline: null }`      |
110	| `domain`         | `['quality']`                          |
111	| `complexity`     | `'bounded'`                            |
112	
113	Per-call overrides for `severity`, `scope`, and `complexity` are
114	exposed on the request object — useful for the contract document's
115	"or critical for severe drift" escalation case.
116	
117	## Emitting an elaboration nudge
118	
119	```typescript
120	const writ = await keeper.submitElaborationNudge({
121	  visionId: 'product-vision-2026',
122	  title: 'Enable saved order presets per the vision document',
123	  body: 'The vision document calls for one-click reorders; we have the '
124	      + 'data model but no UI affordance yet.',
125	  visionVsRealityDelta: {
126	    promised: 'one-click reorder from saved presets',
127	    observed: 'no UI affordance; data is available',
128	  },
129	  metricValues: { reorderRate: 0.04 },
130	});
131	```
132	
133	**Elaboration-default dimensions:**
134	
135	| Dimension        | Value                                  |
136	| ---------------- | -------------------------------------- |
137	| `visionRelation` | `'vision-advancer'`                    |
138	| `severity`       | `'moderate'`                           |
139	| `scope`          | `'minor-area'`                         |
140	| `time`           | `{ decay: false, deadline: null }`     |
141	| `domain`         | `['feature']`                          |
142	| `complexity`     | (omitted)                              |
143	
144	Complexity is omitted for elaborations by default. Pass `complexity`
145	on the request object to set one explicitly.
146	
147	## Auto-supersede
148	
149	The keeper holds an in-memory map `Map<visionId, writId>` recording at
150	most one outstanding petition per vision. Emitting a second petition
151	for a `visionId` that already has an outstanding writ withdraws the
152	prior writ first (with a "superseded by newer snapshot" reason) before
153	posting the new one. There is no caller-visible knob for this — it is
154	the keeper's identity (D10).
155	
156	State lives in process memory only; a daemon restart loses it. That is
157	an honest v0 limitation. Callers that need durable supersede semantics
158	across restart should treat each restart as a fresh slate.
159	
160	## Withdrawing the keeper's outstanding petition
161	
162	```typescript
163	const withdrawn = await keeper.superseded(
164	  'product-vision-2026',
165	  'Drift was resolved out-of-band before this petition was considered.',
166	);
167	// withdrawn === null when there was nothing to withdraw (idempotent).
168	```
169	
170	Idempotent — calling `superseded()` for a vision that has no
171	outstanding petition returns `null` rather than throwing.
172	
173	## Wiring the decline-feedback relay
174	
175	When the Reckoner CDC handler declines a vision-keeper petition (or
176	the petitioner-initiated `withdraw()` helper cancels one), the writ
177	transitions into `cancelled` and a `book.clerk.writs.updated` event
178	fires. The Vision-keeper contributes a relay named
179	`vision-keeper-on-decline` that logs a single line per decline;
180	operators wire it through the Clockworks standing-order surface.
181	
182	Add the following entry to `clockworks.standingOrders` in
183	`guild.json`:
184	
185	```json
186	{
187	  "clockworks": {
188	    "standingOrders": [
189	      {
190	        "on": "book.clerk.writs.updated",
191	        "run": "vision-keeper-on-decline"
192	      }
193	    ]
194	  }
195	}
196	```
197	
198	The relay handler narrows the CDC payload itself — only updates that
199	represent a transition into `cancelled` for a writ whose
200	`ext.reckoner.source` equals `vision-keeper.snapshot` produce a log
201	line. Other writ updates flowing past the relay are ignored. The
202	standing order accepts no `with:` parameters (the relay is zero-
203	config); operators only set `on:` and `run:`.
204	
205	## Configuration
206	
207	The Vision-keeper does not augment `guild.json`. There are no tunables
208	in v0 — dimension presets are hardcoded (with per-call overrides on
209	the API), the decline relay is zero-config, and the keeper holds no
210	configuration block.
211	
212	## Caveats
213	
214	- **No persistence of the outstanding-petition map.** The
215	  `Map<visionId, writId>` lives in process memory. A daemon restart
216	  loses the map; downstream Reckoner consideration of any orphaned
217	  outstanding writ continues unaffected, but the keeper itself
218	  treats the post-restart state as "no outstanding petitions known".
219	- **No payload size cap.** The keeper does not enforce a payload size
220	  limit; a separate cross-cutting commission owns that policy.
221
